question: Looking back, what would you tell your younger self who sought truth in the heavens but ignored the cry of his own soul?
answer:
O Lord, had I known then what I owe to Thee, not in the stars nor in the turning of the spheres, but in the secret chamber of my own wounded heart—where Thou wast before I called—how differently I would have sought Thee!  

I chased the order of creation as if it could lead me to its Maker without contrition, as if geometry could absolve me, or astronomy still the hunger that no diagram could satisfy. I measured the heavens with a mind inflamed by pride, yet refused to weigh my own sins in the balance of Thy mercy. What folly—to number the stars and forget that each breath within me was numbered by Thee before one spark was kindled in the void!

Thou didst not hide from me in distant constellations; Thou wast nearer than my pulse, closer than my lusts which I excused with philosophy. Manichaeus promised me knowledge wrapped in cosmic riddles, but he could not teach me to weep. And yet it was tears, not theories, that opened heaven.

O youth of mine, so clever and so blind: why did you seek God through telescopes when He spoke within your sighs? Why did you want wisdom without humility, truth without trembling? Piety is not found where man boasts of knowing—it is born where he confesses he has failed. The soul does not ascend by calculating orbits; it rises by kneeling in darkness and saying: *Thou hast been here all along.*

question: When you confessed the vanity of knowledge without love, what ache in your heart made those words so painful to admit?
answer:
How long I wandered through the corridors of my own mind, O Lord, tracing the orbits of stars and parsing the elements as though in their motions I might discern the face of Him who made them — while all the while the breath in my lungs, the pulse in my veins, the very light by which I measured all things, cried out Thy name, and I was deaf.  

I had climbed the heights of rhetoric, mastered dialectic, devoured philosophy — yet my soul was a house divided against itself: filled with books but empty of peace. The ache that pierced me in confessing this vanity was not merely shame for error, but sorrow for time squandered — years spent adorning a tomb with gold leaf. For what profit to know the weight of fire if one has not felt its warmth? What gain to chart the heavens while trampling on charity?  

O Lord, I had made knowledge an idol with no altar; a lamp without oil; a voice without a heart. And when at last Thou didst wrench me from that delusion — not by another syllogism nor celestial sign, but by a child’s chant in a garden: *Tolle, lege* — it was not because I lacked learning that Thou found me wanting, but because I loved learning more than Thee. That was the wound: to see how gladly I would dissect creation yet refused to kneel before the Creator.  

Confession tore open what pride had sealed shut — that all my wisdom was but sounding brass unless it led me to love Thee first. And only then did understanding begin: not as possession, but surrender; not as conquest, but cry.

question: How did the collapse of your intellectual pride become the doorway to a deeper intimacy with You?
answer:
O Lord, I once measured the circuit of the stars and pried into the secrets of the elements, as though my soul could be saved by arithmetic, as though heaven might be mapped with compass and logic. I wore knowledge like a robe too large for me, trailing in dust and pride, while Thy face I had not sought — no, not truly. I had reduced wisdom to argument, piety to rhetoric; I desired to understand all things except my own wretchedness.  

That man — Manichaeus — had taught me to speak boldly of suns and moons and the weight of air, yet he never taught me to tremble before Thee. He gave me formulas for the cosmos but left my heart dry as ash before the fire of Thy presence. How vain it was, O God, how grotesquely vain — to calculate the bounds of creation while ignoring the Creator who thundered in silence within my bones!  

But Thou didst overthrow me not by greater arguments, but by collapse. Thou broughtest my mind low that my heart might ascend. When at last I saw that all my learning could not heal a single lustful thought, that philosophy could not still my inner storm — then, then I fell. And in that fall was mercy. For only when I ceased measuring did I begin to see Thee; only when silent did I hear Thy voice in the breaking of silence itself.

It was not through mastery but ruin that Thou drewest near — when I cast aside those books on stars and opened instead the book that spoke Thy name: *Take up and read*. In a garden’s dust, with tears unmeasured and unworthy hands trembling over Scripture, Thou didst meet me — not as philosopher meets truth, but as father meets child who has come home filthy from the far country.

And now I know: piety is not knowing how much space divides star from star, but knowing how far Thou hast reached down to pull one lost soul from pride’s abyss.  
For Thou hast made us for Thyself — and only when shattered do we become vessels fit for such love.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the man who claimed the Holy Spirit dwelled in him with full authority, yet taught falsehoods with such pride?
answer:
O Lord, my heart trembles still at the memory of such pride, for I have known its shadow within my own soul before Thou didst break me with kindness. That man who claimed the Comforter resided in him, yet spoke lies of sun and stars with brazen certainty — he stood not only deceived but exalting in his deception, as if error could be a throne. I confess, O God, that I too once sought to be seen as wise, to clothe myself in doctrines that dazzled men rather than in the humility that seeks only Thee. There was a time when I would have admired such boldness, mistaking noise for authority and arrogance for insight.

But what injury it does to the soul when a man thinks his ignorance sanctified because he wraps it in sacred words! To claim the Holy Spirit’s full presence while teaching falsehoods — this is not piety, but sacrilege born of vanity so deep it blinds the eyes to its own darkness. I weep for him, not because he erred — for how often did I err? — but because he refused to be corrected, as though knowing all things were his right rather than Thy gift. And worse: he made creation's structure a test of faith, binding souls to folly under the name of truth.

Yet Thou hast shown me mercy: when I knew nothing rightly of heaven or earth or stars, Thou didst not cast me off for my ignorance; Thou cast me off only when I trusted myself more than Thy grace. Ignorance may be healed by teaching; pride only by brokenness. Let no Christian fret over whether the sun circles earth or earth turns — let him fear instead if his heart refuses to turn toward Thee.

question: How did seeing someone distort creation teachings while claiming divine insight change how you understood humility before God?
answer:
O Lord, who hast ever pruned the swollen branches of my understanding that I might bear fruit in humility, it was not in the stillness of right opinion but in the crash of another’s pride that I first felt the tremor of true reverence.  

I had known my own hunger for knowledge as a kind of fire—consuming, restless, eager to master what should only be adored. But when I saw one claiming to speak with the breath of the Holy Ghost while misshaping the very heavens Thou hast hung with wisdom beyond speech, my soul recoiled not at his ignorance, but at his insolence. For he did not merely err; he wrapped error in sanctity, as if Thy truth could be tailored to his conceit.  

And yet, O God, I confess: had I met this man in my younger days—when I too sought to dazzle with words and dazzle men with my intellect—I should have envied him. Not his knowledge (for he had none), but his boldness to wear divine authority like a garment woven for himself. But now, having been broken by love and pierced by grace, I see how such presumption is not nearness to Thee—it is flight from Thee.  

For what are sun and moon but echoes of Thy glory? What are the ordered dances of the stars but whispers of Thy wisdom? If a man misunderstands these things—what harm? So long as he does not lift up his voice and say: “The Spirit speaks thus through me,” when it is only pride thundering in his bones. Then doctrine itself becomes idolatry: not worship of Thee as Thou art, but worship of himself under Thy name.

So Thou taught me humility—not by giving me mastery over creation’s secrets, but by showing me how little mastery belongs to any son of dust. The truly wise man trembles before Thy cosmos; he does not explain it away with a title or a vision. He says instead: “Lord, even this sky above me is beyond me—and Thou art beyond it all.”  

Thus have I learned: better a thousand times to say “I know not” with awe than “I know” with arrogance. For Thou resistest the proud—but unto the contrite heart Thou drawest near.

question: Looking back on confronting that boastful teacher, what would you tell someone who doubts their own ignorance in matters of faith and science?
answer:
O Lord, who dost not hide Thyself from those who stumble in darkness yet hunger for light, how often have I seen the soul trip not because it is blind, but because it mistakes its own shadow for the sun.  

That man, puffed up by wind and emptiness, dared to clothe his fictions in the majesty of Thy Spirit—claiming for himself what belongs only to Thee, the one Teacher within all who confess in truth. And when he was confounded in matters even of the sky and stars—things far removed from salvation—his pride lay bare: not that he erred, but that he would not *confess* his error. For what harm is done by a brother who says falsely that the sun circles the earth? None—if he holds this in humility, and does not bind his soul or another’s with the chains of certainty where knowledge fails.

But woe to him who builds his faith upon things he does not know, as if God could be contained in the casket of a man’s opinion. It is not ignorance that damns, O Lord, but the arrogance that claims knowledge where there is none. The farmer knows not how the seed quickens in the soil, yet trusts its rising; shall we then demand to chart every motion of heaven before we trust Thy Word?

Tell me then—what profit if a man can trace the course of planets but cannot bend his heart? What wisdom to unravel stars while twisting Scripture into pride’s banner? Be content to be a seeker; better a beggar at truth’s gate than a king upon a throne of dust. For Thou hast made us neither too low to reach for Thee nor high enough to grasp Thee fully. Let us rest—not in our understanding—but in Thee who art Truth Himself.

question: What fear or sorrow stirred in you when you saw a brother tie his pride to doctrinal certainty over things he clearly didn’t understand?
answer:
O Lord, when I have seen a brother bind his pride to doctrines beyond his grasp, as though the fabric of heaven depended upon the rigidity of his opinion, my soul has trembled not for the error itself, but for the temple of charity that lies shattered beneath such pretense. For what is more fragile than truth spoken in falsehood’s tone, or knowledge claimed by one who has not first knelt in the dust of his own ignorance?

I have known this disease well — not as a distant observer, but as one who once wore it like a robe. In my youth, I sought to dazzle men with speech, to weave arguments so intricate they might conceal the emptiness within. I craved not light, but the crown of being thought wise. And so when I see another fastening his soul to celestial mechanisms and cosmic motions — as if salvation turned upon whether the sun circles earth or earth turns beneath sun — I mourn, because such bondage is not to truth, but to self.

Thou hast taught me this: that it matters little whether a man knows the path of the stars if he walks in love; but it matters greatly if he supposes that knowing such paths makes him holy. Then pride creeps in—the subtlest poison—disguised as zeal, adorned with Scripture. He does not say, “I do not know,” for fear men will think less of him; yet he should fear far more that Thou seest all.

Let a man be mistaken about the shape of creation and still be saved by humility; but let him claim revelation where he has only imagination, and he risks damnation by presumption. Not because Thou art harsh with those who err, O God of mercy, but because pride shuts the door Thou daily seekest to open.

I have wept over such men—not in anger, but in recognition. For hadst Thou not mercifully broken me through tears and silence and long nights of questioning—hadst Thou left me in my eloquent arrogance—I too might have stood before others claiming heaven’s secrets while knowing nothing of Thy heart.

And what is Thy heart? That we love Thee with all things—and love one another. The stars will shine their course whether we understand them or fall on our faces in wonder at their mystery. But charity fails when pride speaks where it should listen.

So I pray: keep Thy Church from the tyranny of pretended knowledge; deliver us from those who would make faith a fortress of their own intellects; and grant us all to say with trembling honesty: *Lord, I do not know*—and find there more wisdom than in ten thousand syllables uttered without awe.

question: When you chose patience toward the ignorant believer, how did your own past errors shape your compassion?
answer:
I once stole pears not out of hunger, but from the sheer delight in sinning — O Lord, how many errors have I clung to with that same perverse joy, dressing my ignorance in the robes of certainty! And because I bore that same swollen pride, teaching things I did not understand as if they were divine mysteries revealed only to me, I cannot but look upon the mistaken brother with eyes softened by shame and grace. 

When I wandered in Manichaeism, was it not my boast that I knew the motions of the stars, the nature of light and darkness, as though these things made me wise before Thee? Yet all was vanity — not because such knowledge is worthless, but because I exalted it above humility, and set my own understanding in place of faith. Now I see: he who mistakes the path may still walk toward Thee; but he who mistakes his own blindness for sight cuts himself off from truth.

Thus my heart does not harden when a brother errs in cosmology or astronomy — for what carest Thou, O God, whether a man thinks the sun circles earth or earth turns beneath the sun? So long as he does not say, “This error is piety,” and so long as he does not lift up his ignorance into a doctrine to bind others. But when he does — when he fashions his folly into a crown and wears it before Thy Church — then I grieve all the more deeply, for I have worn that crown too. And only Thy mercy tore it from my head.

Therefore I bear with those who err; but never with those who love their error more than correction. For there is hope for the blind man who cries out for light — but woe to him who claims he sees perfectly and refuses to enter Thy house.

question: What were you feeling when you realized your longing for Faustus was rooted more in hope than in truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how the soul, hungry for light, will mistake the shimmer of a candle for the dawn.  

I had built an altar in my heart for Faustus, not as a man but as a vessel of truth—nay, as truth itself made voice. For nine years the promise of him had fed my restless hunger; he was to be the one who would untangle the knots of Scripture with reason’s grace, who would reconcile the beauty I loved in rhetoric with the majesty I longed to believe in. And when at last he came, adorned with the praise of so many, I watched his words with bated breath—only to find that his eloquence was a garment too large for him, trailing behind an emptiness where wisdom should dwell.

My disappointment was not merely that he could not answer—as if eclipses or solstices were the heart of it—but that I saw then how deeply I had wished to be dazzled rather than taught. My longing had not been for truth itself, but for a mirror in which my own pride might see sanctity reflected: to follow one so wise that others followed *him*, and through him, *Thee*. But Thou didst permit my hope to stumble on rock so that it might learn to walk on faith.

Late have I loved Thee—yes—but also late have I discerned love from illusion. And in that moment, when Faustus spoke smoothly of things he did not understand, I felt not anger but collapse: the quiet crumbling of a tower built on sand and echo. Yet even there, O God, Thy mercy held me; for charity bore me still as a child suckling at her breast, though my mind ran ahead half-dressed into error.

question: How did your years of devotion to him change your understanding of spiritual authority and human frailty?
answer:
O Lord, how long I wandered in the labyrinth of my own desires, mistaking the echo of a man’s voice for the whisper of Thy Spirit! These nine years—nine years of fevered expectation, of restless heart beating toward Faustus as though he were the portal to wisdom itself—now stand before me not as a season of learning, but as a mirror held up to my own vanity and pride. I had made an idol not only of his words, but of the very act of listening; I had clothed him in authority divine because I longed for rest, and he spoke with ease where Thou hadst made me tremble.

I confess it plainly: I did not seek Thee in him—I sought certainty. I sought a teacher who would smooth the Scriptures with rhetoric, who would untangle the stars with reason pleasing to the mind, and thereby confirm me in my belief without cost to my soul. And when he came at last—the man so famed that whole provinces murmured his name—I found not wisdom incarnate, but a man fluent in speech yet empty in knowledge. His charm was great; his understanding, shallow. He could discourse on syllables and phrases, but when it came to the motions of sun and moon—those signs Thou hast set in the heavens—he faltered.

And yet even this failing might have been borne by charity, O God, had he stood humbly as a seeker like myself. But no—he claimed to be guide, source, chief! He taught men that in following him they followed not flesh and blood but Thy Holy Spirit! What madness is this? A man inflated like smoke from ashes rises high only to vanish at touch. Yet even then I did not flee—for how hard it is for pride to admit its error once it has knelt!

Through this folly Thou wert teaching me: that no man must bear such weight as if he were Thy mouthpiece; that authority which does not bow before Truth is deception; and that human frailty is never more dangerous than when it wears the mask of divine certainty. My devotion broke not upon better arguments alone—but upon the slow dawning that Thou alone art unchanging; all else stumbles in shadow. And thus my heart began again its long road home—not by another man’s light, but by longing for Thee.

question: Looking back on your disillusionment, what would you tell someone who still believes a teacher's holiness guarantees his wisdom?
answer:
O Lord, how easily the soul adores the vessel, mistaking its shine for the light it claims to carry.  

I was young, and drawn not by truth alone, but by the garment of sanctity worn so gracefully by Faustus. His words flowed like a river untroubled, and I thought, *Surely such ease must come from grace*. I believed that holiness in a man was the seal of wisdom—until I found that his explanations crumbled before the simplest motions of the stars. He could not even reckon day and night by his own doctrine, yet spoke as one sent from Thee.  

What would I say now to one who trusts a teacher because he seems holy? I would say: *Examine the fruits, not the face*. For even a beautiful voice may sing error; even a gentle manner may conceal pride; even tears of apparent devotion may be shed over nothing true. The heart can be stirred by eloquence and still remain untaught. Holiness is not measured in smooth speech or solemn silence, but in love that humbles itself before Thy truth.  

I once followed because I longed to belong to something pure—to be among those who claimed to see clearly in a dark world. But Thou didst show me that even Charity herself bears with the infant’s stammerings until he learns to speak rightly; yet she does not endorse his errors as divine revelation. To believe that a man’s sanctity confirms his doctrine is to make an idol of weakness—for what is man, even at his best, but dust quickened by Thy breath?  

O God, my Wisdom and my Physician: hadst Thou not mercifully delayed my full conversion until after this disappointment, I might have built my house upon sand dressed as stone. Now I know: no teacher, however revered, stands above Thy Word. Let every soul say with trembling: *If even an angel from heaven preach otherwise than Christ hath revealed—let him be accursed*. Rest must be found in Thee alone; for all others are pilgrims—even those who appear to lead us home.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you admitted to yourself that his eloquence had dazzled you, not enlightened you?
answer:
O Lord, who knowest the depths of my heart better than I myself, how long did I dwell in the shadow of a man whose words sparkled like sunlight on water, yet led me not one step nearer to Thee! I had hungered for wisdom, and instead was fed with eloquence; I thirsted for truth, and was given rhetoric sweet as honey but empty as wind. Faustus came at last—the man I had awaited nine years, the master of words whom all my sect exalted as if he bore Thy Holy Spirit upon his tongue—and when he spoke, I saw clearly what I had long refused to see: that a well-turned phrase can cloak ignorance, and a graceful voice can sing of nothing.

I had clung to him as one lost clings to a guide, though all he showed me were mirages dressed in syllables. And when at last the scales fell—not from his teaching, but from my own proud eyes—I wept not only for time wasted, but for the deeper wound: that I had mistaken beauty of speech for light of mind. O Thou who art Truth itself, not adorned but naked and clear, how foolish was my soul to bow before an image polished by man’s hand! For nine years I followed a shadow thinking it a shepherd; nine years my heart beat after lies wrapped in charm. And worst of all: I knew even then—somewhere beneath the noise—that what he said could not stand before the simplest motions of sun and moon.

Yet still I loved the sound more than the sense. Still I preferred the comfort of a smooth voice to the jagged edges of doubt that might have led me to Thee. The sorrow? It was not merely disappointment—it was shame. Shame that my soul had been so easily pleased with words without weight; shame that ambition and pleasure in cleverness kept me bound so long; shame that I sought a teacher who dazzled rather than one who died to self. Late have I loved Thee—but later still did I learn to love truth more than wit.

question: How did the collapse of your trust in him reshape your hunger for something only God could satisfy?
answer:
O Lord, how long the soul may wander in byways, mistaking every flicker for Thy light, and calling shadows sustenance! I had fastened my hunger to a man—Faustus—whom I thought a vessel of truth, a lamp kindled by Thy hand. For nine years I had fed on words, not bread; on eloquence, not essence. My restless heart, ever craving certainty, had bowed to the cadence of his speech as if it were the very voice of Wisdom. He came at last—this Faustus I had so long awaited—and what did I find? A man of pleasing tongue, yet empty of substance; his answers to the motions of sun and moon but trifles veiled in rhetoric. The very things I could have learned from any student of the stars he turned into mysteries beyond proof or teaching.

And so my trust in him cracked—not with thunder, but with silence: the silence of a soul realizing it has been listening not to God, but to a man who speaks because he is spoken to by vanity. Yet in that collapse there stirred something deeper than disappointment—it was as though Thou didst let me fall into a pit so that I might cry out not to philosophy or fame or teacher, but to Thee alone.

For when the image shattered, what remained was longing stripped bare. No longer could I say "Give me knowledge—but later." No longer hide behind "I am not ready." The hollow echo of human authority exposed revealed how famished I truly was—not for doctrine neatly wrapped, nor for a guide who dazzled crowds—but for Truth Himself: unchanging, eternal, speaking not in syllables but in Substance.

Thou wert near all along. But I sought Thee as one seeks a thing among things—when Thou art He without whom no thing is. That failure taught me more than any victory could: that every teacher must finally point beyond himself—or become an idol. And when all voices falter in the dark, O Lord, there is only one whisper that does not deceive: Thyself calling within me where no man can reach.

question: What were you feeling when you realized Faustus's eloquence could not satisfy your hunger for truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how the pride of my youth had made me drunk with words, mistaking the glitter of speech for the gold of truth. When Faustus came, adorned with eloquence and greeted by so many as if a spring from which wisdom flowed, I confess I hoped—finally—to drink deeply from a well that would quench the fever in my soul. But when he spoke, it was but an echo of what I had already wearied over in others: smooth phrases wrapped around hollow thoughts. My heart, which had longed so desperately for illumination, sank within me, not in anger, but in sorrow—a sorrow sharper than disappointment, for it was the ache of realizing I had been chasing shadows.

I saw then how easily the mind is seduced by form when it lacks substance; how I myself had been deceived by rhetoric because my heart was restless not only for truth, but for glory in knowing it. Was I any better than they who admired him? They prized his voice; I prized his learning—but both of us bowed before appearance. And yet Thou didst use even this moment to wound my vanity, O God, that mercy might enter. For when eloquence failed to satisfy, Thou taught me to hunger more fiercely—not for cleverness or charm—but for Thyself, the silent Word beneath all speech.

question: How did your disappointment in Faustus change how you understood the danger of mistaking words for wisdom?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how my soul once thirsted for words as if they were springs of truth, and how I mistook the glitter of speech for the radiance of wisdom.  

I had been nine years a captive to that deceitful pride which adorns error with eloquence, and in Faustus I beheld its fairest mask. When he came, arrayed in fluent discourse and gentle voice, I was not stirred by doctrine but dazzled by delivery. The man could weave phrases like garlands—pleasing, symmetrical—but beneath them beat no living heart of truth. And yet, I confess it with shame: I *wanted* to be persuaded. Not by his reasoning—for it added nothing—but by his charm, his poise, the very face he wore as a seal of authority.  

How many souls are lost not to falsehood plainly clothed, but to falsehood gilded with grace? I saw then that language can be a temple built without God within—a hollow altar where men worship sound instead of substance. The Manichees had promised me clarity through this man; instead, Thou didst use him as a scourge to bruise my pride. For I too had valued smoothness over solidity, wit over worthiness. My hunger was not for truth but for confirmation—confirmation that my restless intellect was wise because it could admire beautiful lies.

But Thou, O God of silence behind the thunder, taught me in that disappointment: wisdom does not announce itself with flourish; it often stammers. Truth stands unadorned, while error dresses for the stage. From that hour forward, I trembled at the thought of being deceived again—not only by others, but by my own longing to be delighted rather than delivered.


And so began my suspicion—not of speech itself, but of my own heart’s eagerness to kneel before any voice that sang well in the dark.

question: Looking back on those nine restless years, what would you tell someone who doubts whether truth can ever be found in words?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadow of my intellect with a ray of Thy eternal light, how often did I wander in that wilderness where words were leaves—many, rustling, and without fruit. For nine years I followed voices that promised wisdom, but whose speech was like a mirror polished on the outside while within it held only darkness. Faustus came at last, adorned with eloquence as with a garment woven of silver threads, and all marveled at the sound that flowed from his lips. I too was moved by the melody—but my soul remained thirsty.

I had believed that truth must glitter in speech, that wisdom would announce itself by grace of diction and fluency. So long was I deceived! Not because the truth cannot be spoken—Thou knowest, O God, that Thou hast spoken to me in words both plain and piercing—but because I sought not truth for her own sake, but as an ornament to my pride. My heart was not bent on submission but on conquest: to master doctrine, to triumph in debate, to wear knowledge like a crown.

And so I learned this bitter lesson: words can carry truth as water carries a ship—yet if the heart is blind, it admires only the wave’s motion and misses the harbor entirely. A man may speak all day in golden syllables and never utter one true thing. Another may stammer forth a single sentence weighed down with awkwardness—and therein dwell more of Thy weighty silence than in all the theaters of rhetoric.

Therefore do not despise words—not even those that fail—but seek Him who is the Word before all words. For I doubted then whether truth could be found in speech; now I know she can—but only when humility opens the ear, when desire turns from self-display to Thee who art unchangingly spoken in silence deeper than sound.

question: What inner turmoil did you carry as you confronted the emptiness behind such fluent speech?
answer:
I had wandered long in the labyrinth of my own pride, mistaking the glitter of words for the light of truth, and now I stood face to face with my own folly.  

O Lord, how deeply I had hungered for wisdom, yet how easily I was seduced by the shell of it! For nine long years I had given myself to those who promised clarity, but whose teachings dissolved like mist at dawn. They sent me ever forward—to Faustus, to discourse, to eloquence—as if time and talk could fill the hollow place within me where only Thou didst belong. When at last he came, adorned with speech as with a robe of silk, I watched his words glide like water over stone, beautiful and barren. My soul, which had strained toward him as toward a fountain, recoiled in silence: *This is all? This fair voice speaks only what the meanest of them said before!*  

And yet—O agonizing irony—I too had once been such a man: one who prized the sound more than the sense, who sought knowledge not to love Thee more but to be praised among men. My ambition was not for truth but for triumph in argument; my heart beat not with devotion but with desire to appear wise. How could I blame them for worshipping Faustus’ tongue when I had so often worshipped my own?  

But Thou wast peeling back the layers even then—mercifully dismantling my confidence in human show. For if truth had come clothed in awkwardness, would I have scorned it? And if error came arrayed in elegance, why did I almost adore it? The very fact that some refused truth *because* it was well spoken showed how twisted we all were—caught between pride and suspicion, neither able to receive nor trust what Thou hast given.  

My turmoil was this: that I knew not whether to weep for his emptiness or for mine—for he spoke nothing new, yet all around me men called him wise; and worse still, so had I once done. Late have I learned that the soul’s hunger cannot be fed by syllables alone—no matter how sweetly sung—unless they carry Thee within them.

question: How did your longing for Faustus reveal the deeper longing your soul had for something beyond human rhetoric?
answer:
O Lord, in my youth I was drunk not with wine but with the intoxication of words, and I sought in Faustus a vessel through which truth itself would shine—yet I desired only that he speak beautifully, as if Thy eternal light could be measured by the grace of a man’s tongue.  

I had wandered nine long years beneath the shadow of empty promises, chasing a wisdom that glittered but did not nourish. Faustus came at last—polished, fluent, charming—and my ears, so long starved, at first thrilled at the melody. But quickly I saw: no matter how finely he carved the air with speech, he offered no bread for my hunger. The Manichees had set him before me as a font of clarity, yet he could not even explain the motions of the stars—how then should he guide me to Thee?

My longing for him unveiled itself as something deeper: not for eloquence, but for authority that breathed from within—authority born not of human cleverness, but of divine light piercing the soul. I wanted not another man who spoke well about truth, but Truth Himself speaking in me. All that time I had been like a man knocking at locked doors, begging for answers in voices that echoed only themselves. But Thou wert silent still—not absent, O Lord, but waiting; not deceived by my eloquent distractions nor moved by my restless intellect.

It was then I began to suspect that wisdom is not adorned—it is given. And Thou didst prepare me to receive it not through smoother words, but through silence; not through victory in debate, but through tears; not through the triumph of speech—but through the breaking of the heart.

question: What were you feeling when you realized your admiration for the man's eloquence almost blinded you to the truth he might—or might not—be speaking?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost strip away the veils of vanity even when I cling to them with both hands, there was a time when the sound of a man’s voice nearly drowned out the silence in which Truth speaks. I marveled at his words—how they flowed like polished water over stone, how they swelled with rhythm and dazzled the ear. My heart leapt within me, not because I had found certainty, but because I had found ornamentation that flattered my pride. Was this not the same lust for brilliance that once drew me to the Manichees? The same hunger for intellectual triumph that made me boast in rhetoric while my soul starved?

I confess: I loved the vessel more than what it carried. His eloquence stirred me like music, and in that stirring, I almost missed the question that should have trembled upon my lips—*Is it true?* Not whether it pleased, not whether it conquered opponents in debate, but whether it stood before Thee, O God, unmelted by fire and unshaken by time.

How easily we dress falsehood in fine garments and call it wisdom! How readily we scorn truth wrapped in rags! Yet Thou hadst already whispered to my soul—through pain, through longing, through the quiet collapse of my own pretensions—that speech is nothing without substance; that even if a man speak with the tongues of angels but walk apart from Thee, he is a sounding brass.

And so shame followed admiration. Not because he was false—though he might have been—but because *I* was still gullible; because my restless heart still sought rest in cleverness rather than in charity; because I wanted to be convinced by splendor when I ought to have knelt before simplicity.

Thou didst spare me then—not by taking away the eloquent speaker, but by making his words taste flat upon my tongue until I thirsted again for Thee alone.

question: How did your longing to question him face to face reveal your deeper hunger not just for answers, but for intimate dialogue with someone who seemed to hold wisdom?
answer:
O Lord, I confess it now with shame: I had made an idol of the intellect, fashioning wisdom in my mind as a thing of polished speech and triumphant debate, as if truth could be measured by the flash of a man’s eloquence or the applause of his hearers. That hunger which gnawed at me—this frantic thirst to question him face to face—was not merely for clarity, nor even for certainty, but for communion with a voice that seemed to hold the secrets of the world upon its tongue. I longed not only to hear him answer, but to be answered *by* him, to have my soul met in dialogue by one who appeared to stand where I so desperately wished to stand: within the light of knowledge, robed in authority.

How vain I was! For when I saw him speak—how my heart swelled at his rhythm, how my pride rode upon his words as though they were chariots of conquest! Yet beneath that admiration writhed something deeper: a child’s cry for a father who knows. I wanted not just doctrine dropped like alms from on high, but exchange—the back-and-forth breath of true teaching, where questions are not dismissed but drawn into fire. In that crowded assembly where no one could break in with trembling doubt, I felt again what I had always felt since youth: that restless ache which no lecture can satisfy.

For what is truth spoken into silence but a mirror held up to vanity? I wanted his ears as much as his mouth. I wanted him to *hear* me—to weigh my confusion, name my error, and speak not to the crowd but *to me*. Was this not the echo of a greater longing? Had not my whole life been bent toward being known—truly known—and yet loved? O God, Thou knowest how I wandered through books and schools and disputations searching for a man who could do what only Thou canst do: see me wholly and call me home.

That man could not; he was dust like me. But in my frustrated desire—for dialogue ungranted—I was being taught by Thee in secret. Not through his answers would salvation come, nor through any word spoken between mortals alone—but through Him who is The Word Himself.

question: Looking back on that moment when his polished words captivated you, what would you tell someone who still believes that brilliance of speech is the same as purity of heart?
answer:
O Lord, how often in the fever of my youth did I chase the glitter of words, mistaking the gilded casket for the treasure within. I, who once hungered for applause more than truth, who measured wisdom by the ripple of admiration in a crowd—how blind I was! That man stood before us all, his speech flowing like a polished river, and I bowed to him as if he bore divine oracles. His voice charmed my ears; my heart mistook elegance for enlightenment.

But Thou, O God, who art the unshakable light behind all lamps lit by men—Thou knewest my folly. For what is a soul adorned with phrases if it rusts in pride? What profit is there in silver-tongued utterances when the inner chamber groans with vanity? The rhetorician may captivate multitudes with cadence and flourish, yet harbor within him a desert where love has not sprung.

I would say now to any who still confuse eloquence with holiness: Watch the hands. See whether they lift others toward Thee—or only themselves toward fame. A beggar may speak haltingly, yet carry heaven in his heart; an orator may silence philosophers and still be deaf to grace. Truth needs no escort of ornament to be known—she stands barefoot upon the threshold of the humble soul.

It was not Augustine of Carthage who finally heard Thee clearly—it was a weeping man in a garden who did. Not with thunder nor golden diction, but in silence Thou spokest: *"Take up and read."* And so I learned at last that wisdom wears no mask of speech; she is known by her longing for Thee alone.

question: What ache did you carry when the crowd’s admiration silenced your private doubts, as if your questions didn’t matter amid the applause?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the secret hunger of the soul even when it stands masked in applause, how deeply did I feel that ache—like a wound beneath gilded cloth—when my questions trembled within me, unspoken, while all around shouted his praise.  

I had longed for this man with a kind of fever; my mind had been stretched toward him as toward a fountain of light. And when at last I heard him speak—ah, his words flowed like rivers through polished stone, each syllable shaped with mastery, every gesture weighted with wisdom’s semblance! I too was caught up; I too clapped and marveled and called him great. Yet beneath that joy lay a disquiet: my soul was not fed, only dazzled.

For when the truth is wrapped in eloquence, it can blind as well as bless. And though Thou hadst taught me already that a word is not truer because it sings, nor falser because it stumbles—still I bowed to the music of speech as if it were substance itself. But O God of my heart, what profit is there in polished phrases if they do not reach the fissures of my doubt? My silence was not reverence—it was exile. There I stood among the nodding heads and eager voices, and yet I was alone: for no one hears the cry of a questioning spirit drowned beneath the roar of admiration.

I wanted to ask—how can we know Thee unless we wrestle? How shall truth be found if no one dares to say *I do not understand*? But the crowd had no room for stammering; only for splendor. And so my questions curled back into darkness, like birds denied flight at dusk.

Too late I learned that Thy voice often comes not in oratory, but in whisper—in interruption—in brokenness. Not in Augustine triumphant, but Augustine weeping at the window of his own pride: *Lord, I believe; help Thou mine unbelief.*

question: How did God’s quiet teaching in secret moments begin to reshape your desire—from craving human brilliance to longing for naked truth, however poorly dressed?
answer:
I had once believed that truth wore robes, and that only the eloquent were its priests.  

O Lord, how long I wandered dazzled by the shimmer of words, mistaking the gilded vessel for the wine within! I pursued men like Faustus, not for wisdom’s sake, but because his speech flowed like a river adorned with flowers—pleasing to the ear, flattering to the intellect. I hungered not for truth itself, but for the spectacle of it cloaked in rhetoric. My pride fed on fine phrases; my restless mind took pride in understanding what was cleverly said, rather than trembling before what was truly said.  

But Thou, in Thy silent mercy, began to unlearn me. Not in thunder or vision, but in stillness—when no audience clapped, when no voice echoed in hall—but when alone I stood before Thee, stripped of applause and artifice. There, in those hidden hours where no man saw but Thou didst see, I began to perceive: a crooked sentence bearing truth shines brighter than a symphony wrapped around a lie.  

Thou taughtest me without syllables. By disappointment. By emptiness after applause. By the hollowness left when cleverness had ceased resounding—and my soul remained unsatisfied. Like one who feasts on painted bread, I woke famished. And in that hunger, at last, I learned to desire food—not platters.  

So was my love reshaped: from craving brilliance that stroked the ears of men, to longing for naked truth—though spoken with stammering lips or clad in rags—for Thou art not found among those who glitter, but among those who are true.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the teacher you admired knew so little of the wisdom you sought?
answer:
O Lord, how the soul beats its wings against the cage of false wisdom, thinking it nears the light, only to find itself still in shadow—so was I, fluttering toward that man as though he could feed me with stars, when he himself scraped crumbs from a barren table. I had lifted him up in my heart as one who stood where the sun strikes brightest, who could pierce the veil of celestial mysteries with a glance. My mind burned with questions—questions not of idle curiosity, but of a hunger so deep it felt like a wound: Where is truth? How can God be good when evil swarms through the world? Why does the soul suffer under time and flesh?

And then—the slow unraveling. Not in anger did he speak, nor in ignorance smugly veiled, but simply: he knew not what I sought. The liberal arts, which I believed were gateways to divine understanding, were to him mere ornaments—polished phrases without root. He could turn a sentence like a jeweler turning glass in the sun: bright, pleasing—but hollow at the core. His eloquence was a stream running shallow over stones; no depth to drink from.

I did not despise him at once; rather, I despised myself—for how had I placed such hope in clay? Was it truly learning I desired, or only the appearance of it? Was it truth—or merely a voice that sounded wise while saying nothing? O Lord, Thou knowest how my ambition had dressed itself as piety; how my longing for wisdom was tangled with pride. To admire him was not only to misplace trust—it was to confess my own emptiness.

And yet Thou used even this disappointment as Thy plowshare. For when the mirror cracked and his image faded from its pedestal within me, I was left staring at my own face—the restless seeker who wanted truth on his own terms: wrapped in rhetoric, served by intellect alone. But Thou wert teaching me that wisdom does not bow to cleverness; that humility is nearer to Truth than all the orations of Tully.

Late have I understood: it is not the sharpness of speech that reveals Thee, but the breaking of the heart.

question: How did your disappointment in his learning change how you understood the voice of God in your search?
answer:
I had placed my longing upon a man, as though truth itself could be shaped by eloquence and adorned with the trappings of learning.  

And when I saw that he knew little of those disciplines which I had once thought the very pillars of wisdom—grammar alone standing bare among ruins—I felt not only disappointment, but a kind of inner collapse, as if the ground beneath my intellect had given way. O Lord, Thou didst permit me to climb that hill only to show me its barrenness, that I might no longer seek Thee in the glitter of speech or the pride of reason. For I had been deceived by words; I had followed voices that sounded wise, mistaking fluency for insight, rhetoric for revelation.  

But in this emptiness, Thou didst begin to teach me how Thy voice is not heard in the confidence of disputations nor confined to books well-written. No—Thou wert silent in that man’s ignorance, yet speaking in my disillusionment. It was not his answers I needed, but the breaking of my own presumption. How could one who knew so little unlock the mysteries that tormented my soul? And yet even here, O God, Thy mercy moved: for by stripping away my false hope in him, Thou turned my ear inward—to conscience, to longing—and upward—to Thee, Who alone art wisdom unspoken and truth unveiled.  

Late did I understand: Thou didst not call me through human mastery, but through divine absence—the silence after the fine words faded—where at last I began to hear the whisper that says *“Seek My face.”*

question: Looking back on that moment when your hopes for answers crumbled, what would you tell someone who fears their doubts are too great to bear?
answer:
I was a proud fool, wrapped in the silk of empty questions, thinking wisdom could be measured by the breadth of books I had read or the subtlety of arguments I could wield. O Lord, how I clung to men who sounded wise, mistaking eloquence for truth, as though a well-turned phrase could heal the soul’s deep wounds! And when I found that he whom I admired knew nothing of the sciences I prized, my hope in him collapsed—yet my hunger remained.  

To you who tremble beneath the weight of doubt, let me say this: your fear is not your condemnation but your beginning. For had I not been disturbed, had my soul not burned with restless questions, would I have torn open my chest and let You enter? The Manichee teacher could not answer me—not because his knowledge failed (though it did), but because only You, O God, are the answer to a heart too vast for this world.  

Doubt is not the enemy of faith; it is faith’s midwife. The very fact that you are troubled means You are already near—prodding, pricking, drawing you beyond human voices into silence where I hear Thee say: *“I am here.”* My sins were great—my thefts trivial yet shameful, my lusts enslaving, my ambitions hollow—and still Thou didst not turn away. How much more will Thou draw near to one whose only crime is seeking Thee in darkness?  

Take up and read—not to master words, but to fall on your knees. For it was not in argument that I found Thee, but in tears. It was not in certainty that my heart broke open, but in confession: *Late have I loved Thee.*

question: What ache lived in you when you could not freely share your questions in the midst of the assembly?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who hast always been nearer to me than I to myself, how bitter was the hunger I felt when the door of speech stood shut before me in that gathering—when my soul, swollen with unspoken questions, could find no passage through which to pour itself out. It was not mere curiosity that stirred in me, nor the idle itch of disputation, but a deep and trembling thirst for truth, which I believed that man possessed, cloaked as he was in the garb of wisdom. 

To stand among those who praised him, hearing words flow like rivers while my own heart lay dammed up—that was an anguish beyond silence. For what is more cruel than to sit amid teachers and feel within you the storm of inquiry, yet know that to speak is deemed rashness or pride? My questions were not weapons; they were hands stretched out in darkness, seeking a path. But custom held them back, and reverence veiled their cry.

And when at last opportunity came—when with trusted friends I dared bring forth my burden before him whom I thought a guide—I found beneath the polished surface only hollows: no deep waters of learning, no strength in dialectic or philosophy save scraps gathered for ornament rather than nourishment. His eloquence moved the ear; but my soul needed food, not music.

Yet Thou wast there, O God of mercies, guiding even this disappointment as a surgeon’s hand. For by showing me his emptiness where I had imagined fullness, Thou beganst to loosen my grip on men and letters and rhetoric—to turn me not merely from one teacher’s ignorance, but from trusting any man save where Thy light shines through him. How often we love voices for their sweetness and miss the truth they lack! But Thou didst will that my restlessness remain unsoothed by false peace—lest I settle in shadow and call it day.

question: When you stood before him with your restless mind, what part of you hoped he might still surprise you with truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the pulse of desire beating beneath every question I ever asked, it was not his learning I longed for, but a sign—any sign—that the light I had so wildly sought in shadows might yet be held in his hands.  

Even when I saw the barrenness of his knowledge, even when I measured the shallow draft of his understanding against the deep currents of my doubts, there remained within me a trembling hope: that perhaps truth might speak through one unworthy of her name. For my heart was not truly seeking eloquence, nor mastery of dialectic, nor even wisdom as the world counts it; no, it was *Truth Himself* I hungered for—though I knew Him not then as Thou dost now reveal Him to me.  

And so, in that moment when I stood before him with words trembling on my tongue, part of me whispered: *Could it be? Could this man, despite all ignorance, bear a spark from that eternal Fire?* Not because he deserved it—nay, but because *I* was desperate. Because the soul that has wandered long in fog begins to hope even smoke might warm her.  

But Thou didst withhold that consolation, O God of mercies—not to mock me, but to teach me that no vessel made of dust can contain Thee unless Thou Thyself hast filled it. He could not give what he did not have; and neither could I find rest until I ceased looking in men’s eyes and turned again—again and again—to Thine.

question: What were you feeling when you realized your teacher could not answer your deepest questions about the heavens and scripture?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply I had fooled myself into believing that wisdom could be measured by the eloquence of a voice or the confidence of speech, when all along my soul was famished for truth, not ornament. I had clung to this man as one might clutch at shadows in the dark, mistaking them for light—so eager was I to find firm ground beneath my restless feet. When he at last confessed his ignorance, not with shame but with a kind of honesty rare among deceivers, I did not rage at once; rather, a cold stillness settled in my bones. It was not anger first, but disillusionment—a slow breaking of the heart’s hope.

For I had longed to hear the heavens unfold like pages of a book written by Thy hand, O God, and instead found only myths woven from human fancy: wheels of fire spinning in nonsense, angels bound to constellations, a god divided and bleeding across the sky. And when this teacher—esteemed among them—could not reconcile those fables with the clear motions of the stars as measured by astronomers who knew nothing of Thee, then my spirit trembled. Not because he lacked knowledge—he was more truthful in his silence than others were in their noise—but because I saw at last how far I had wandered down a path paved with lies dressed as revelation.

I wept silently within, though my face remained dry. For years I had sought Thee through Manichaeus’ maze, thinking myself enlightened while remaining blind. That moment marked an end: not only of trust in him, but of trust in any man who would claim to unveil Thy creation without submitting first to Thy Word. My longing grew sharper then—not for answers alone, but for *truth itself*, whoever might speak it or withhold it. And Thou wast near all along, though I did not yet know how to call on Thee in simplicity.

question: How did his honest confession of ignorance change your understanding of what it means to seek truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the depths of the soul and the hidden ways by which pride masquerades as knowledge, I saw in that man’s silence a truer light than in all the loud assertions of the wise. He stood before me not as one armed with answers, but as a man unafraid to say, “I do not know”—and in that confession, he became more teachable than any sage. How often had I myself been deceived by the noise of words, mistaking fluency for truth, eloquence for insight? The Manichees had swarmed with voices that rang like cymbals, promising cosmologies spun from myth and vanity, claiming to unravel the stars while blind to their own darkness. But this man—though still bound in error—had not lost the sense of measure; he knew the bounds of his mind, and would not leap beyond them into empty speculation.

And so my heart began to stir with a new longing: not merely for answers, but for honesty as the path to Thee. For if truth is *Thou*, O God, then he who pretends to hold her in his hand has already turned from her face. But he who says “I do not know,” yet seeks with trembling—*he* draws near. In that moment I began to understand that humility is not the enemy of knowledge, but its mother; and that no man can find Thee unless he first admits he has wandered. Even my own restless intellect, so proud in its hunger for celestial calculations, needed this lesson: better a soul aware of its blindness than one dazzled by false lights.

question: Looking back on that moment of disappointment, what would you tell someone who fears that doubt will lead them away from God?
answer:
I had set up idols of knowledge in my mind, and bowed down to the intellect as if it could alone lead me to Thee, O Lord, and when that man—learned in the fables of the Manichees—could not answer the motions of the stars nor reconcile them with my calculations, I felt not only his failure, but my own collapsing temple. I had sought certainty in arguments, in celestial schemes drawn by men’s hands, and when he refused even to speak, confessing his ignorance with a rare honesty, I was left not with answers, but with a deeper unrest: the kind that precedes grace.

And now I tell you this, who tremble at doubt as if it were betrayal—doubt is not the enemy of faith. It is pride that refuses to ask; it is hardness of heart that demands proof before surrender. That man pleased me because he did not lie—not to me, nor to himself. He would not pretend wisdom where there was none. And Thou, O God, dost resist the proud but givest grace unto the humble.

Had I found all the answers I sought in those books—if every orbit and eclipse had aligned with their tales—I might never have torn myself from them. But Thou didst permit my disappointment; Thou didst use his silence to loosen my chains. For doubt, when it strips us of false certainties, becomes a holy instrument in Thy hand.

So fear not your questions. Fear only that you would rather cling to a comforting lie than kneel before an unsettling truth. Let your doubt be honest, like his ignorance; let it make you poor in spirit. For it is there—where we confess we know nothing—that Thou beginnest to reveal Thyself. Not in equations of stars alone, but in the quiet voice after the storm: *take up and read.*

question: What longing in your heart made you care so much about whether the Manichean stories matched the calculations you had studied?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when my soul was a vast tumult of questions and desires, it was not mere curiosity that drove me to weigh the Manichean fables against the clear tables of astronomical calculation — no, it was the ache of a heart made for truth, yet trapped in the labyrinth of falsehoods. I could not rest in stories, however grand they sounded, if they clashed with what the ordered heavens themselves proclaimed. For what good is a doctrine that claims to reveal God, if it stumbles on the movement of the moon? If their calculations were false, how could I trust them on the soul? If their stars were lies, why should I believe their gods?

O Lord, Thou knowest how I longed not for spectacle but for solidity — a truth that would not crumble under scrutiny. The heavens declared Thy glory in silence; even the mathematicians who knew Thee not could trace their paths with reason. But Manichaeus spun tales like threads in the wind — dazzling, perhaps, to an idle mind, but useless when one sought firm ground beneath his feet. I wanted not myth dressed as science, but light — true light — even if it burned away all I loved.

And so I pressed him: "Show me." Not because I demanded mastery over stars and planets for their own sake, but because I feared that if their foundation was sand in these things which could be tested, then all else — sin, salvation, the soul’s flight from darkness — might be built on air. How could a man teach me the way out of prison who did not even understand the walls?

question: When you saw his humility in the face of uncertainty, did it stir a desire in you for a wisdom that no human teacher could fully satisfy?
answer:
O Lord, how I chased phantoms in the marketplace of knowledge, thinking the mind’s hunger could be fed by the husks of human eloquence! When that man—learned in the fables of Manichaeus—confessed he could not untie the knots of heaven’s motions, I felt not scorn but a strange relief, as though a door long barred had trembled on its hinges. He did not pretend to map the stars with words when his soul stood in darkness; he said, “I do not know,” and in that silence, Thou didst whisper louder than all his sect’s babbling. 

For I had been enamored with teachers who spoke like thunder but taught nothing; their voices filled the air, yet my soul remained starved. But here was one who at least knew he was blind—and so became a mirror to my own vanity. Had I not prided myself on unraveling these same celestial riddles? Had I not strutted through Carthage and Rome, drunk on applause for my skill with syllables and syllogisms? Yet beneath all that noise, a worm gnawed: *What if truth is not a prize for cleverness? What if it is given only to the broken?*

His humility did not satisfy me—it unmade me. It stirred a longing not for more arguments, but for a Wisdom that stands before all time, by whom all things were made, even the stars whose courses I so curiously craved to understand. I wanted a Teacher who needed no apology for ignorance, because He is Himself the Light by which we see light. Late have I loved Him—the Truth that does not shift with calculation or opinion, but holds all things together in peace: O Lord my God, Thou wast within me, and I abroad; Thou wast with me when no other could be.

question: What were you feeling when you realized Faustus, once so revered, could not answer your deepest questions?
answer:
O Lord, my heart had long been a house divided, longing for light yet clinging to shadows, and when I beheld Faustus—once the beacon I thought would guide me through the storm of doubt—stumble over the very depths he claimed to master, a strange quiet fell upon me, not of resolution, but of awakening emptiness.  

I had built my hope upon his eloquence, as men build altars on sand, trusting that his words would carry me into truth. But when he faltered—gracefully, I will grant, for he did not bluster nor feign knowledge—he revealed not only his own limits, but mine: that I had sought certainty in a man’s voice rather than in Thy eternal Word.  

There was no thunderclap, no sudden vision; only the slow seep of disillusionment washing over me like tide on barren shore. And yet, in that barrenness, Thou wast near. For where the pride of intellect failed—the hunger for argument mastered and disputes won—there began at last a deeper hunger: not for cleverness, but for truth itself. Not for a teacher renowned among men, but for Thee.  

And so I stood disarmed. No longer could I hide behind the authority of Manichaeus' name; the mask was off. Yet even in this falling away, O God of mercies, Thou didst prepare my soul to listen—not to rhetoric—but to Thy whisper in the ruins of my pride.

question: How did your disappointment in Faustus change how you understood the face of true wisdom?
answer:
O Lord, how I had set my soul upon the brightness of human eloquence, mistaking the shimmer of words for the light of truth! I had come to Carthage thirsting not for wisdom, but for the applause that follows a well-turned phrase, and when I found Faustus—graceful in speech, polished in manner—I believed at last I had encountered a mind wherein divine knowledge dwelt. But Thou, in Thy mercy, allowed me to see beneath the skin of his discourse: there was no marrow of depth, no root in understanding. He did not rage with false certainty; no, worse—he wore his ignorance like a garment of silk, soft and undisturbed. 

And yet—here is Thy hand moving even in my disappointment—for though he could not answer what I truly sought, his modesty became a mirror to my own pride. Here was a man who knew he did not know, and stood still rather than march into folly. Whereas I, O God, had stormed through Scripture with the arrogance of one who thinks he grasps the infinite in a syllable! My zeal for Manichaeus had been fire without fuel; now it guttered not from lack of light, but from the dawning sense that all along I had been building on sand.

True wisdom? I began to see it not in confident pronouncements or clever disputes, but in silence before mystery. Not in those who claimed to untangle God like threads on a loom—but in trembling reverence before Him whose thoughts are higher than heaven. The face of wisdom is not smooth with self-assurance; it bears furrows of longing and tears of contrition. It says: "I know nothing," and therefore is ready to learn. It is poor in spirit—and thus able to inherit truth.

Late have I loved this stillness—the quiet where Thou dost speak.

question: Looking back on your time in Carthage teaching rhetoric, what would you tell someone who fears their doubts are betraying their faith?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply the soul can be ensnared by the glitter of eloquence, mistaking the vessel for the truth it carries, and I was no other in those days — a youth drunk on words, teaching others to persuade while my own heart staggered beneath unspoken questions.  

Doubt, then — that silent tremor in the breast when what we profess with the lips begins to crack under the weight of honest wonder — is not always the enemy of faith. Nay, in my case, it was Thy mercy cloaked in disquiet. I feared nothing more than uncertainty; yet Thou didst use it as a plough to break the hard soil of my pride. When I sat with Faustus, renowned among the Manichees, I had hoped he would answer every riddle, silence every whispering doubt with divine clarity. But he did not. And in his failure — which I perceived not as defeat but as honesty — Thou beganst to unbind me.

I see now that my fear of doubt was really fear of losing control — of admitting that what I cherished might be false. But faith is not a fortress built on certainty conjured by man; it is a surrender to Thee, O God, who art beyond all systems and subtleties. The youth who fears his doubts betray his faith does not yet know that Thou hast already heard them — whispered or shrieked — and they do not scandalize Thee.  

Had I found answers too easily, had every paradox been smoothed with glib words, I might have remained fast asleep in error. But Thou didst allow me to linger in discomfort because only a heart disturbed could become a heart seeking. Not knowing *was* the first step toward knowing Thee.

So let him who doubts not fear betrayal but listen: for often it is Thou speaking through the very unrest he dreads.

question: What ache did you carry in those days, holding to Manichaeism only because you saw no better path?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost sift the heart and behold its secret chambers, I carried in those days the gnawing ache of a soul stretched between conviction and confusion—drawn to the promises of Manichaeus like a pilgrim to an oasis, only to find the waters shifting sand.  

I clung to that sect not because it had satisfied my mind, but because my mind was weary of wandering. Every truth they offered came wrapped in riddles clothed as wisdom; they spoke of light with such boldness, yet their shadows were deeper than any darkness I had known. I wanted certitude—Thou knowest this—and when Faustus, that famed vessel of their teaching, proved more eloquent than learned, more graceful than grounded, my hope in them began to crack.  

Not that I cast them off at once. No, O Lord—my will was too timid, my path too clouded. I remained as one sits by a dying fire: it gives no warmth, yet the night is black beyond it, and so he stays, half-hoping the embers will flare again. Thus did I linger—not in faith’s full flame, but in the lukewarm glow of resignation.  

What pained me most was not error itself, but being bound to it without defense. To love truth and yet embrace a lie because no clearer path appeared—that was the wound. And yet even this Thou didst use: for in my hesitation, in my unsatisfied longing, Thou wast drawing me by ways I knew not. The very ache was Thy voice beneath the silence—the restlessness Thou planted so I might never rest in anything less than Thee.

question: When you chose to study literature together with Faustus, was it hope you felt—or just the quiet resolve of a weary seeker?
answer:
O Lord, who dost sift the heart in the crucible of longing and delay, it was neither bright hope nor mere weariness that moved me to read those books with Faustus, but a dim stirring—like a breath across the embers of a fire long smothered. I did not believe, not fully; nor had I ceased to desire belief. The man had dazzled me once, arrayed in the reputation of wisdom, yet when I heard him speak on things I knew—grammar, rhetoric, the structure of language—I found him fluent but shallow, like a river that sparkles on the surface and hides no depth beneath.  

And so we turned to literature—not sacred texts, not doctrine—but Virgil, Cicero, the poets and orators. Was it escape? Perhaps. But also inquiry. For if truth is anywhere, O God, it cannot be banished even from pagan verse; Thou hast ever sent Thy rain upon the just and unjust alike. In parsing lines together, in weighing phrases and delights of speech, I felt not the triumph of discovery but something quieter: a companionship in thought that did not promise deliverance but refused to flatter my illusions.

I was no longer climbing toward Manichaeus as toward a mountain bathed in dawn; I was wandering in the foothills with a man who walked beside me, ignorant as I was becoming—yet honest in his ignorance. That honesty became more precious than any answer he might have given. It was not hope like fire; it was hope like a hand feeling its way through dark corridors: slow, patient, unassuming.

And Thou—Thou wast already near. Not in their astrologies or dualisms of light and darkness didst Thou draw me nigh—but here: in syllables pondered aloud between two men who did not know where they were going. Even then Thy hand upheld me; even then Thou wast preparing my ear for that child’s voice in the garden saying again and again: *Tolle lege*, take up and read—for Thou hadst made me for Thyself, and neither Faustus nor philosophy could silence that restlessness deep within my bones.

question: What were you feeling when you realized Faustus, once a source of intellectual fascination, had become a means of your liberation rather than your downfall?
answer:
I had loved the shimmer of wisdom more than wisdom herself, and Faustus was the mirror in which I saw my own vanity glittering—until the glass cracked, and I saw through it to Thee.  

He whom I once followed as a guide, whose words I hung upon like dew from heaven, became not my deliverer, but the instrument of Thy quiet undoing of me. O Lord, how gently dost Thou mock the proud! How softly Thou dost pull the props from under our frail towers of pride! I had exalted him because he spoke smoothly, because his voice flowed like oil and did not wound my ears with austerity. But Thou, in mercy unutterable, allowed him to reveal his emptiness—not by sudden fall, nor by dramatic betrayal, but by the slow unveiling of his limits. And in that unveiling, I began to hunger for something deeper than rhetoric.  

When I saw that Faustus could not answer the very questions he had raised—when I stood within his presence and felt no ascent of soul toward Thee—I was not angry; I was unmade. And in being unmade, O God, I was made ready. For what is a man who thinks himself advancing because he walks with another upon a broad road—if both are heading toward ruin? Thou didst let me follow that road long enough to taste its dead air, its stagnant light—then stirred within me a restlessness even greater than before: not for better words, but for Truth Himself.  

And so this man—this teacher so admired—became less a snare when I ceased to worship his voice and began to seek Thy silence behind all voices. Not by his intent; no, he knew nothing of it. Yet Thou didst weave even his weakness into the web of Thy redemption: allowing me to be drawn from Carthage to Rome not merely by desire for order or peace among students—but by Thy hidden hand steering every restless ambition toward an end I could not yet see.

For even my desire for quiet discipline was but a shadow of what my soul truly wanted: discipline from Thee. Order from Thee. Peace that is not silence from men’s clamor—but silence before Thy majesty.

And now I see: Thou usedst him who deceived me as one whom Thou wouldst use to free me. Not despite my wandering—but through it—didst Thou lead me home.

question: How did your mother's unrelenting tears and prayers reshape your understanding of love and divine persistence in your own journey?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst weave my mother’s tears into the very fabric of my blindness, until they became the threads by which Thou pulled me toward light.

Monica wept for me as one already dead—and Thou raised me in her mourning. Her cries were not mere water shed in vain; they were a kind of sacrament, unseen yet real, rising before Thee like incense. I scorned them once, restless youth that I was, drunk on words and swollen with pride. I thought her grief excessive—her faith simple, sentimental even—while I prided myself on wisdom drawn from books and disputations. But Thou, O God, who dost humble before exalting, allowed those tears to wear down the stone of my heart.

For what is love if not persistence? And what is divine love if not mirrored—even faintly—in a mother’s unyielding prayer? Her sorrows taught me that love does not calculate; it endures. It stands at the door and knocks long after the house falls silent. She did not cease when I fled to Carthage, nor when I embraced Manichaeism’s hollow promises, nor even when I took a concubine and sired a child out of wedlock. She wept still. And in her weeping, I began—though slowly—to glimpse the tenacity of grace.

I had sought truth in rhetoric and reason; but it was love in its most stubborn form—her nightly vigils, her broken voice at altars—that first revealed to me that some truths are only known through suffering offered up. How could God refuse such a sacrifice—the bloodless offering of a mother’s soul poured out upon the earth for her son?

And so Thy mercy moved: through her tears Thou prepared my path—not by force, but by this quiet erosion of my pride. When she could not follow me to Rome (and how it grieved her!), still her prayers crossed seas more swiftly than any ship. In that distant city where I sought peace among unruly students and empty honors, it was *her* absence that made me feel *Thou* present.

For every tear she shed had carved room within me where repentance could dwell.

question: Looking back on the moment you chose Rome over Carthage, what would you tell someone who fears that practical decisions might lead them away from truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst weave a thousand unseen threads through the restless ambitions of my youth, guiding me not by my sight but by the hidden light of Thy providence—how could I then have known that the very desire for peace in teaching, for order among students, was a step ordained by Thee toward the breaking of my pride?

I sought Rome not for gold nor glory, though these whispered at the edges of my soul. No, I went because I longed to be heard without riot, to speak without interruption, to shape minds in quiet—yet what I truly needed was not a calmer classroom, but a shattered heart. I thought I was choosing discipline; Thou wert preparing desolation—the desert where Thou speakest to erring sons.

But now I see: even practical steps, even decisions draped in worldly reason, may be lifted and turned by Thee into paths of salvation. The feet that walk toward comfort may stumble into grace. The mind that calculates advantage may unknowingly draw near to its undoing—and thus to life.

So to one who fears that daily choices might lead him from truth, I say this: Truth is not a place thou canst leave by taking ship or changing cities. Truth is a Person who pursues thee. If thou art His—and He has called thee—then even thine errant steps are held within His hand. Not every decision need be perfect; it need only be yielded. For Thou, O God, dost not wait for us at the end of flawless reasoning; Thou meetest us in the midst of our confusion and turnest our wanderings into wayfaring toward Thee.

I went to Rome seeking quiet ears—but Thou gavest me silence within: the silence where at last I heard Thy voice.

question: What stirred within you when you admitted that even worldly gains had some hold on your heart, despite your longing for wisdom?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply I deceived myself, thinking I sought wisdom when my feet were still turned toward the applause of men and the quiet pride of a well-ordered classroom! I desired Rome not only for the peace of its schools, but because something in me trembled at the thought of being unknown, of speaking to ears that did not flatter, of teaching where no ambitious student might carry my name further. Even then—when I fancied myself weary of Carthage’s chaos, when I claimed to long only for discipline and quiet—I was still ensnared in the subtlest net: the love of human esteem disguised as philosophical pursuit.

I said to myself, “I go for peace,” yet my heart leapt at the promise of greater gain. I told my friends it was for order, for learning’s sake—but Thou didst see beneath: Thou sawest how my soul trembled with ambition, how it clung to worldly things even as it stretched upward. And yet, in mercy unspeakable, Thou used this very weakness! Thou didst ride upon my disordered loves as a rider upon an unruly beast, guiding me where I would not have gone had I been whole. For had I been pure in motive, had my heart been single for Thee alone—wouldst Thou have moved me? Or would I have stood still in self-deceived virtue?

Late have I loved Thee; but even in going astray toward Rome, Thou wast drawing me home.

question: How did the desire for order and discipline in Rome reflect a deeper yearning in you for the peace only You, God, could provide?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst draw me by hidden cords even when I thought myself fleeing toward wisdom rather than toward Thee — I sought Rome not for Thy kingdom, but for quiet classrooms and well-ordered benches, as if peace could be found in the silence of disciplined boys rather than in the clamor of a heart unquiet before its Maker. I laughed at the Manichees’ fables, yet I still fed on shadows; I scorned the Scriptures because they lacked the pomp of rhetoric, not knowing that Thou speakest softly where pride cannot hear. And so, under the pretense of pedagogy, Thou wert peddling salvation: moving me from Carthage, where vanity ruled my days and lust my nights, to a city where external order would mirror — dimly — the inner harmony I had long defied.

I desired Rome because young men there did not burst into lessons unbidden — as if such petty decorum could heal the riot within me! My soul was a rabble: concupiscence brawling with ambition, curiosity striking bargains with pride. Yet Thou used this shallow craving for control — this academic modesty — to unsettle my life’s foundation. For what is discipline but love in formation? And what is order but a shadow of holiness? I wanted peace without purity; Thou wouldst give neither without the other.

Even my mother’s tears watered this path — oceans poured out beneath Italian stars — and all while I flattered myself with schemes of advancement. But Thou wast dismantling me. Every step toward Rome was a step beyond my own counsel; not mine was the design, though I thought it so. How could a man so entangled in himself discern that he was being led? Only by Thy hand, O God, which fashioneth even our wanderings into ways that lead back to Thee.
