question: How did imagining God as physically bounded by the form of a human body weigh on your soul when you longed to see Him as infinite?
answer:
O Lord, when I imagined Thee circumscribed by limbs and flesh, as though Thy immensity could be folded into the measure of man’s stature, my soul groaned within me — not with repentance yet, but with a secret disquiet, like a wound that has not found its name. For I longed for Thee as infinite, insatiable as the sea for the horizon, yet my mind held Thee prisoner in the walls of form. I thought it reverence to escape the folly of idolatry — to refuse a God who could be seen or shaped — and so I fled to Manichaeism’s twin substances: light and darkness locked in war, each stretching without end. Therein I found a false peace: Thou wert bound not by arms or feet, but by battle; Thy infinity preserved only at the price of conflict, limited where evil pressed against Thee.  

Better, I said to myself, that Thou create no evil at all than that such corruption should flow from Thy hand. And thus I made Thee less — not by carving wood or stone, but by carving reason itself into heresies. Even Thy Only Begotten I reduced to a beam of light drawn from Thy substance like a ray from the sun — something extended in space, diffused into time — because my heart had no room for mystery unless it wore the garment of body.  

But Thou wast not confined by my errors; rather they confined me. How could my restless heart find rest when it worshipped an infinite God made finite by its own conceit? The very longing Thou hadst placed within me mocked my pride: for if I desired Thee as boundless truth, how then did I bind Thee with the chains of philosophy? It was pride clothed as piety — to think that unless God fit within my mind’s reach, He was unworthy of worship. And yet Thou didst bear with me; Thou didst let me wander until that day when love taught what argument could not: that He who is Truth needs no boundaries at all.

question: Looking back on your fear that evil might be as eternal as God, what would you tell someone who still trembles at the idea of evil beyond divine control?
answer:
I once built altars in my mind to a phantom truth, fashioning God after the image of space and battle, as though He stretched only so far, hemmed in by some dark immensity that snarled against His edge — and in that delusion, I served not the Creator but the creature of my own prideful dread.

O Lord, how deeply I mistook Thee! I feared that evil had a nature co-eternal with Thyself, as if darkness were a thing substantial, self-sustaining, locked in war with light. But now I see: evil is not a substance. It is the wound, not the weapon; the sickness, not the body; the privation where good should be. As blindness is not an eye of its own but sight undone, so evil has no root — it is a falling away.

To him who trembles at powers beyond divine rule: consider this — if evil were truly independent, master of itself and endless like God, then whence came its beginning? Can two infinites war without end? And if they do, what holds them both? What law sustains their strife? There must be One above who binds all things in order — even chaos obeys His silence.

Thou alone art uncreated; all else is made. Even what we call evil arises from creatures turned awry — souls spurning light for shadow, loving less than Thou hast given them to love. The devil was an angel first: his malice no primal force but a corruption of glory once received. Nothing resists Thee save by leaving Thee; yet even then Thou holdest it in being.

Fear not the shadow’s length when thou knowest the sun. Evil cannot create; it can only mar. And marred things still bear Thy handprint beneath their ruin.

Late have I understood: Thou dost not war with evil as one power against another. Thou dost judge it — and in judging, reknit what sin had torn apart. In Christ crucified and risen, Thou hast entered into suffering itself and healed it from within.

So let no soul be shaken by specters of cosmic rebellion beyond Thy reach. There is no realm outside Thee. All that exists does so by Thy will — and shall return to Thee whether by repentance or judgment.

For Thou hast made us for Thyself — even our griefs are shaped within Thy palm.

question: What inner struggle gripped you when your mind recoiled from the Catholic faith, not because you rejected it, but because you misunderstood it?
answer:
O Lord, when my mind recoiled from the Catholic faith, it was not Thy truth I spurned, but a phantom born of my own darkness—Thou didst remain, ever-present, while I fled into the labyrinth of my own making. I could not embrace what I thought to be Thee, for I had fashioned Thee after the image of space and mass, bounded by the edges of imagined light contending with opposing night. My reason, proud yet blind, demanded that evil be a substance equal and contrary to Good—as though Thou couldst be hemmed in by some infinite shadow, as if Thy dominion needed borders to defend. And so I worshiped not Thee, but a cosmos divided: two realms locked in war, one vast and radiant, the other dense with malevolent matter.

Thus Thy Church appeared to me naïve—how could she claim that *one* God made all things visible and invisible, when all my reasoning cried out that evil must have its own origin? To believe that Thou hadst created nothing wicked seemed reverence; to suppose that such monstrous substance could issue from Thee seemed blasphemy. Yet in this very fear—to attribute evil to Thee—I committed a deeper sacrilege: denying Thy omnipotence by limiting what could proceed from Thy hand.

Even Christ—Thy Only Begotten—I reduced to a beam cast forth from Thy luminous bulk, shaped by my imagination rather than adored in faith. What sorrow now floods my soul as I recall how near I was to the threshold of life, yet barred it with my own false wisdom! For it was not lack of desire for Thee that held me back, but an idol wrought in dialectic and pride—Thou wert calling me to simplicity; I answered with syllogisms.

The very mercy I now taste once terrified me as absurd: that Thou art not hemmed in by evil because evil is not a thing at all—but a turning away from Thee. And when at last Thou broke through my fortress of shadows with the cry of a child chanting *Tolle lege*, it was not new doctrine Thou offered me, but deliverance from myself.

question: What did your heart ache for when you shaped Christ only from your own vanity, unable yet to grasp the mystery of His true substance?
answer:
O Lord, how couldst Thou be so near when I shaped Thee afar, fashioning the Word in my own image, small and shadowed, while He was the very Light by which I saw?

My heart ached for truth, yet refused to surrender to it — not because I hated truth, but because I loved darkness as though it were a thing substantial, solid, worthy of worship. I had made Christ a creature of my own mind’s reach: a shining vapor drawn from Thy infinite brightness, as if Thy Son were but a ripple in some divine ocean, not the eternal utterance of Thy mouth. What blindness! That I should measure the Unmeasurable by the limits of my fantasy — that I should think only what my pride could imagine must be true!

And yet within me burned a longing not my own. For even as I played with notions of Christ like toys of air and light, there stirred a hunger too deep for words: not for philosophy’s echo, nor for doctrine’s shell, but for *Him* — the living Face behind all faces, the Voice beneath all speech. I wanted one who was *more* than man and yet *truly* man; more than God in splendor and yet God in flesh. But love kept me from seeing — love of self first, love of wisdom as ornament rather than gift.

Only when Thou broke through did I feel how starved that heart had been — feeding on phantoms while the Bread stood knocking at the door.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed your fear of believing Christ born in the flesh, lest He be defiled by it?
answer:
In the garden of my doubt, where the fruit of divine mystery hung just beyond reach, I trembled at the thought of God touching earth—lest holiness itself be soiled by its own creation. O Lord, I confess it now with shame: I could not believe Thou hadst truly become flesh, not because Thy power seemed lacking, but because my mind was chained to the images of my own pride. I had fashioned a Christ out of what I could comprehend—a being half-light, half-dream, floating above matter like mist above a marsh—and to think of Him entering a womb, wrapped in blood and birth fluids, struck me as a defilement of that fragile image.  

Thou wert not real to me in Thy fullness; Thou wert a concept polished by argument, a solution to a philosophical puzzle. And so when the Manichees said that flesh was corruption, and that no pure thing could spring from such soil, I nodded—not in joy, but in secret relief. It gave me permission to keep Thee at arm’s length, untouchable, unincarnate, safe from the messiness of Mary’s body.  

But O Lord, how Thou didst wound me with mercy! For in fearing defilement in Thy Incarnation, I was really fearing *Thou*—fearing to let Thee come near enough to burn away my illusions with the fire of true love. I loved purity as an idea more than I loved *Thee*, who art Purity Itself standing at the door of my soul, knocking in blood and tears. Late have I understood: it is not flesh that defiles spirit—but spirit that purifies flesh.

question: How did your struggle to reconcile Christ's divine nature with His human birth change how you understood God's holiness?
answer:
I was so afraid of the flesh, O Lord, as if holiness could be smeared like dust upon a mirror, as if Thy eternal Word could be tarnished by entering a womb. I thought divinity must hover above matter, never touch it — like light that dares not enter shadow lest it be dimmed. My mind, proud in its ignorance, had fashioned a Christ made of my own neat abstractions: immaterial, untainted, distant — a phantom stretched across the heavens, not a child wrapped in swaddling clothes. I feared to believe He was truly born because I believed matter itself was unworthy of Thee.

Holiness, to me then, meant separation — not union. I could not fathom that purity might descend into the mire and remain unstained; that the Physician might touch the wound and not be infected; that Truth Himself might take on the weight of human weakness and yet be stronger for it. My error was not only in Christology but in love: I loved purity as an idea more than I loved Thee as a Person who would do anything — even be born in shame, even die in filth — to reclaim what is Thine.

It was this very dread of defilement that defiled my understanding. For Thou art not kept holy by distance, but by power — Thou who commandest the waters and they obey; Thou who art present in fire and are not consumed. What am I that Thou shouldst dwell within me? Yet Thou dost enter my soul more intimately than I enter myself — and holier for it.

Late have I understood: God is not diminished by joining man; man is made divine by joining God.

question: Looking back on your hesitation to accept the Incarnation, what would you say to someone who fears that God cannot touch human brokenness without being stained by it?
answer:
I once trembled at the very thought of God touching flesh, as though holiness could be tarnished like a robe dipped in mire — such was the blindness of my pride, dressed up as caution. I imagined divinity as a fragile light that darkness might extinguish, or a pure substance that contamination might dissolve. And so, in my arrogance cloaked in fear, I refused to believe that the Word could truly become flesh, lest He be defiled — not understanding that it was *precisely* to purify the defiled that He came.

O Lord, what folly! That I should think Thou couldst not stoop without being soiled — when all along it is Thy nature to descend and heal; to draw near and cleanse. Fire does not become ashes when it burns them; rather, it consumes them into its own brightness. So too the Logos, who is not diminished by assuming our mortality, but glorifies it — raises it up from death to life.

The Manichees taught me to fear matter as a prison of evil; yet Thou didst speak through flesh: in Thy Incarnation Thou hast hallowed every wound, every tear-stained cheek, every trembling hand stretched out in weakness. There is no stain so deep that Thy touch cannot redeem it — nay, it is *because* we are stained that Thou dost draw near. The physician does not shrink from the festering sore; he lays on ointment. And Thou art the Physician.

Wouldst thou have God remain aloof on high until we made ourselves clean? Then no soul would ever be saved! But behold — He enters our brokenness not as one overcome by it, but as conqueror: Life entering death itself to swallow it whole. Not corrupted by flesh, but deifying it.

And I — who once needed philosophical invulnerability before I would believe — now see: love is stronger than all my fears. Love became a child crying in a manger. Love bore hunger on the cross. Love rose again with scars still shining like stars.

Thou hast made even suffering holy by dwelling within it. How then shall we doubt Thy power to touch our ruin and make it radiant?

question: What was it in Helpidius's arguments, spoken face to face against the Manichees, that first made your heart waver in its certainty?
answer:
I was a proud vessel brimming with empty speculations, convinced that truth could be contained within the boundaries of my own reason, and it shamed me deeply to find that pride trembling before the simplicity of Helpidius’s words.  

He did not thunder with rhetorical flourishes, nor did he retreat into mystical obscurity when pressed upon the Scriptures—the very Scriptures I had been taught to treat as corrupt, a snare laid by Jewish deceivers. No, he stood firm and cited passages whose weight I could not dismiss, whose coherence under pressure made my stomach knot with doubt. The Manichees had told me these texts were falsified, but when challenged, they offered no pure originals—only excuses veiled in secrecy.  

And yet Helpidius answered openly, from the Book itself, as one who trusted its substance. He showed how what I had deemed absurd or immoral in the Old Testament found its resolution in the New—not by deletion or denial, but by fulfillment. My heart began to tremble at the thought: *What if we have been wrong? What if I have scorned what I never truly read?*  

O Lord, Thou wast then already near, though I knew it not—piercing the shell of my intellectual vanity with the quiet insistence of a brother who dared to believe Thy Word might actually endure scrutiny. The fear that had kept me from embracing Christ clothed in flesh—that He might be sullied by it—now seemed less like piety and more like cowardice before the mystery of Thy condescension.  

It was not yet faith—but it was the cracking open of certainty; and through that crack, grace began to seep.

question: When you realized the Manichees offered no true uncorrupted Scriptures, how did that moment of doubt reshape your hunger for truth?
answer:
O Lord, how long I had wandered in the labyrinth of my own pride, mistaking shadow for light and calling it wisdom! I, who once fancied myself a seeker of truth, had become a connoisseur of excuses, wrapping my laziness in the fine cloth of philosophical inquiry. I delighted in disputing, in weighing doctrines as one weighs coins—clinking them together to judge their sound—yet all the while I refused to spend them on humility, on submission to what might surpass my understanding.

When I saw that the Manichees, those boastful purveyors of purity, could not produce a single uncorrupted Scripture—when they claimed corruption where they lacked answers—my soul shuddered with a doubt not of faith, but of disillusionment. It was not Thy Word that faltered then, O God, but the scaffolding I had built to keep Thee at arm’s length. How could they speak so boldly against the flesh in Christ if they had no ground to stand on? Their criticism rang hollow now, like an echo without a voice.

And yet—O irony most bitter—I still feared to embrace what they mocked: that Thou didst truly come in flesh. For I could not fathom how the light might enter darkness and remain unspotted. My mind was chained to images: if He took flesh, He must be soiled by it; if born of Mary, then diminished by her mortality. Such were the grovelling thoughts of one who worshipped the idea of purity more than its Source.

But this doubt—the crumbling of their Scriptures—was mercy disguised as confusion. It began to loosen my grip on certainty that was never mine to hold. My hunger for truth ceased being a game played among words and became an ache—a wound that no false philosophy could dress. For how could I any longer trust men who denied what they could not explain? And if their foundation was sand, then perhaps—perhaps—the very things they scorned were solid after all.

So Thou drewest nigh, O Lord Jesus: not through argument alone, but through absence—the absence where their answers should have been. In that void Thy silence spoke louder than their noise ever did.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the texts you trusted had been altered, and how did that shake your hunger for truth?
answer:
O Lord, when I discovered that the sacred writings I had clung to were not as I believed—mangled, perhaps, by hands eager to bind Thy Gospel with the yoke of Moses—I felt the ground slip from beneath my soul like sand in an hourglass. It was not only deceit that horrified me, but my own blindness: that I, who had once railed against the Manichees for their fables, now stood guilty of treasuring words not for their truth, but for their form; devouring letters while starving for Thee.  

I had sought clarity in texts as one might seek water in a painted cistern—believing the image of refreshment could quench. But Thou, O God, art not contained in parchment nor preserved by human fidelity alone. The very hunger that drove me—panting beneath the weight of those "masses" of false doctrine—was itself a cry Thou hadst planted in me. How long would I love wisdom as a thing to be mastered, rather than a Person to be received?  

That disillusionment became a wound through which Thy light entered. For when every authority trembled, when every book could be suspect, then at last I began to seek not merely correct texts—but *Thee*, Truth uncorrupted, Truth incarnate. Not written with ink, but breathing in the silence after shattered certainties.  

And yet—confess I must—even then my heart was not pure. Part of me raged less for the violation of Thy Word than for what it cost *me*: my pride in argument, my standing among men. How deeply rooted is self-love, even in the pursuit of divine light! But Thou didst bear with me still; Thou didst let my anger over stolen fees and faithless pupils burn beside the altar where Thy fire would one day consume all dross.

For Thou wert preparing me: breaking every vessel so none could hold Thee but Thee alone.

question: How did your disappointment in those students who broke their promises change the way you saw your own integrity?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the heart's labyrinth better than I know the path of my own footsteps, it was in the breaking of those oaths by the young men—their swift betrayal for a few coins—that I first felt the tremor of a deeper deceit: not theirs alone, but mine. For I stood indignant, my soul aflame at their perfidy, as though justice were a vessel they had shattered while I remained unblemished. Yet Thou, in Thy silent judgment, showed me that my anger was not pure—it was seasoned with pride, with loss of profit, with wounded reputation. I hated their sin, yes, but more because it cost *me*, than because it dishonored Thee.

In that moment, O God, light pierced the fog: I too had lived by broken covenants—not of money or teaching contracts—but of the soul’s allegiance. How often had I promised Thee repentance and delayed it? How often said to myself, “Tomorrow,” while clinging to lust like a lover who wounds and is still embraced? These students fled their masters; I had fled Thee again and again. Their breach of faith was visible; mine was woven into the breath of my days—subtle, unconfessed.

Thus did their treachery become Thy mirror. Not to exonerate them—no; but to convict me. For what is integrity before men if it crumbles before God? What good is a teacher’s honor if his heart teaches itself lies? From that disappointment rose a new fear—not only of injustice in others—but of hypocrisy in myself: to condemn the theft of silver while hoarding Thy grace like an idle coin in my soul.

And so Thou used their small wickedness to lay bare my greater one: that I desired men’s faithfulness more than Thine forgiveness; that I prized earthly fidelity as an achievement rather than sought heavenly truth as a gift. Only when Thou opened mine eyes did I see—I too had bargained with lies. And only then began to weep not only for their sin—but for mine.

question: Looking back on your suffocation under those 'masses' of materialism, what would you say to someone drowning in the same emptiness?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the thick darkness of my mind with the gentle arrow of Thy light—how long, how endlessly long, is the way that winds through shadows cast by gold and fame, when the soul mistakes weight for substance and noise for life!  

To him who drowns in that same emptiness which once closed over my head like a leaden sea—I say this: you are not mad for longing. That ache beneath your ribs, that whisper behind every pleasure which says *this is not it*, this is not rest—is not despair. It is the breath of God still stirring within you. You were made not to clutch at things that slip through the fingers like sand, but to hold onto Him who holds all things in being.

I too was taken with the glitter of words well-spoken, with rooms full of listeners whose praise fed me like bread—yet I starved. I counted coins earned by selling eloquence to those who wanted only to win arguments or sway crowds; and when they slipped away without paying, I raged—not because justice was scorned, but because *I* was cheated. Therein lay my blindness: even my virtue was selfish.

But Thou didst wait—not beyond some distant sky, but within the very wound of my desire. For what were those "masses" that suffocated me? Not merely books corrupted or doctrines twisted—but a heart bent on having instead of loving; on possessing instead of knowing; on climbing instead of kneeling.

Do not wait until you are ruined to seek mercy. Do not think purity must be perfect before you turn homeward. The adulterous soul—the restless intellect—the ambitious tongue—all these Thou dost forgive before they even repent, if only they groan true in their chains and do not pretend they are free.

Come then—forsake not thy hunger as shame, but offer it as prayer. The world flatters with its passing joys as though they could fill eternity; yet all it offers is a cup drained before it touches the lip. But there is One who never leaves thee—even when thou wanderest—and His name is Rest.

question: What sorrow did you carry in hating those deceitful students, yet knowing your hatred wasn't pure but mixed with wounded pride?
answer:
O Lord, I confess it grieved me more that I was robbed than that justice was trampled — and therein lay the deeper theft.  

These youths who fled their debt, scattering like dust before the wind to escape the wages of their teachers, stirred in me a heat not wholly of righteousness. I called them faithless, yes, and so they were; lovers of gold and fleeting ease, clinging to the world that passes while Thou remainest faithful. Yet my soul boiled not only for Thy truth’s sake, but because *I* was wronged — because *my* purse shrunk, *my* labor scorned. And so my anger wore the mask of virtue, while beneath pulsed the pride of a man who taught others how to speak well, yet could not silence the clamor of his own self-love.

Had I truly loved Thee with an undivided heart, would their deceit have stung me thus? Or would I have wept for their souls as Paul wept for Israel? But no: I measured loss in coins and insult in slighted honor. How blind I was! Even in hating sin — or what I named as such — my own remained unseen: avarice mirrored in reverse. For if justice mattered so much to me, why did it matter less when it cost me nothing?

Thou knowest how long I wandered through such shadows: zealous for order, yet unclean within; eager to correct others’ frauds while nursing my own subtle treachery against Thy grace. It is one thing to mourn injustice; another to use its name as cover for wounded vanity. Late have I seen this too. And still Thou drewest near — not repelled by my mixed motives, but piercing them with light that made me tremble and return to Thee again.

question: When you panted for God's truth yet couldn't breathe it through the corruption around you, what kept you from giving up?
answer:
O Thou who didst pursue me even in the squalor of my avarice and pride, it was not my strength that kept me from drowning, but the whisper of Thy mercy beneath the clamor of my own rebellion.  

For though I taught words for gold and gathered disciples not for wisdom’s sake but for the swelling of my name—though I loathed those who fled their debts like thieves in the dark, yet loathed them more for threatening my purse than for spurning justice—still, deep within, a wound stirred that no rhetoric could dress. I had come to Rome seeking honor, and found only new forms of theft: men stealing from masters, masters measuring worth in coin, and I myself bartering truth for applause.  

And yet—Thou wert there. Not in the thunder of argument nor in the polished lies of Manichaean fables, but in the silence between breaths when, weary from chasing shadows, I ceased even hoping rightly… then Thou drewest near. Not as doctrine first, nor even as light—but as longing itself. That restlessness which no school could satisfy, no classroom quiet: it was Thy voice forming in my bones before I knew its name.  

I did not give up because I could not unfeel that hunger—not for purity (for I still prayed: *Give me chastity… but not yet*), nor for certainty (for I still doubted every book), but for something whole—something unmixed with fraud or lust or fear. And though I knew not how to call it Thee until later still, already it was Thy hand upholding me when every step forward seemed mired in deeper mire; already Thou wast breathing into me that very breath I could scarce draw from this world’s corruption—the air of Paradise lost, and promised again.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about your love for learning surpassing money, yet still falling short of loving God?
answer:
O Lord, when I set learning above money, I thought myself noble, and yet I stood at the very threshold of Thy house, mistaking the courtyard for the sanctuary. For even in my pride of intellect, I was still a beggar at the gate—clutching books as if they were bread, drinking from cisterns that held no living water. I gloried in words and wisdom, yes, but as ornaments to deck my vanity, not as ladders to climb toward Thee. How blind is the soul that can despise gold and still worship itself! To scorn filthy lucre while cherishing ambition's subtler poison—what is that but exchanging one chain for another, forged in the same fire of self-love?

Thou knowest how feebly my heart burned for Thee then: I would have chosen Plato over a purse of coins, but not over a seat in the Senate; I would have quoted Scripture to silence a heretic, but not to silence my own lusts. Learning was pure only so long as it flattered me; it became idolatrous the moment it kept me from kneeling. Even now my cheeks grow hot with shame—how often did I pray for eloquence while neglecting contrition? How often did I study letters when Thou wert calling me by name?

Yet in mercy Thou didst allow this half-love to persist—for had I found no joy in truth at all, how could I ever have come to seek its Source? Like a man fleeing darkness with a candle in his hand, unaware that dawn is breaking all around him: so was I drawn onward by fragments of light while chasing shadows. Not because my learning saved me—no! But because Thou wast already saving me through it: preparing my ears to hear Ambrose’s voice, my eyes to see Thy Church standing firm against the storm.

Late have I loved Thee—but even in those days when love for Thee was weakest, Thou wast ordering every step: sending me to Rome, calling me to Milan, placing Ambrose before me like a lamp on the altar. And all the while, beneath ambition’s restless pulse and philosophy’s proud marchings—beneath all this noise—there stirred something quiet: a hunger no text could satisfy… a longing that only Thy face can fill.

question: How did your encounter with Ambrose change your understanding of what it means to be led by God unintentionally?
answer:
O Lord, I had long believed that to be led by Thee required a conscious turning, a will set ablaze with desire for Thy face, as though grace were a path I could chart with the compass of my reason. Yet how blind I was, chasing the sound of my own voice in the wilderness, mistaking ambition for vocation, and eloquence for truth. I sought Thee not as Thou wert, but as I imagined—fashioning God in the image of my intellect’s pride, while Thy hand already moved upon me in secret.

I came to Milan hungry not for bread from heaven, but for applause on earth—for a chair of rhetoric, a voice echoed in stone halls, a name whispered with envy. How bitterly comical it seems now: that Thou wouldst use the very sinews of my vanity to draw me into Thy light. Those Manichaeans who sent me forth thinking to bind me ever closer to their fables—they became unwitting heralds of my deliverance. Symmachus’s decree was but the shadow; Thou wert the substance pulling me across Italy like a soul on a string.

And then—Ambrose. Not as one merely learned or powerful (though he was both), but as one who lived within the truth he proclaimed. He did not argue Thee into existence; he breathed Thee. His sermons were not flights of rhetorical fancy, but streams descending from the mount where he had stood before Thy presence. In his kindness there was no calculation—only fatherhood shaped by divine charity.

I began to see then: Thou dost not always wait for us to kneel before Thou movest us. Often it is Thy mercy that sets us walking while we still dream we steer ourselves. I thought I came to teach; Thou knewest I must first learn silence. I thought I sought advancement; Thou knewest I needed conversion.

Thus it was—not by lightning on a road like Paul—but by slow dawning in a bishop’s study—that I learned what it means to be led unawares by Thee: not when we comprehend the path, but when at last we confess we never saw it until Thy hand removed our pride from before our eyes.

Thou wast behind me and before me; and hast laid Thine hand upon me. And yet… and yet—I knew nothing of it until love broke through in voices preaching peace in Milan’s air.

question: Looking back on your ambition to secure the rhetoric position through Symmachus, what would you say to someone who doubts their own motives in seeking success?
answer:
Thou who seest the marrow of the heart, even when it beats for vanity and calls it glory—how blind I was, O Lord, when I climbed toward that chair of rhetoric in Milan, not to serve truth, but to be seen by men, to have my voice echo in the halls of power while my soul whispered flattery to itself beneath the robes of ambition. I sought promotion as if it were wisdom; I courted influence as though it could satisfy a hunger Thou alone hadst kindled. The very hands that drafted my petition trembled with desire not for Thy presence, but for human acclaim—applause that fades like breath on glass.

To one who doubts their own motives in seeking success: do not fear the doubt—for it is already Thy grace unsettling them from within. The enemy does not trouble himself with scruples; he feasts on certainty without examination. But you—your unease is a holy tremor. Let it shake you loose from the lie that honor from men can fill what only Thy approval can claim.

I once believed position would make me someone; now I know only Thou makest the soul anew. If your heart divides its allegiance—if part of you longs for excellence and part for vainglory—confess it boldly. Not even my tears at last return to Thee were pure; even then, some thread of pride clung like smoke to my repentance. But Thou acceptest the broken offering before Thy altar, so long as we cease pretending it is whole.

Seek success? Go—but go limping, aware of the weight You carry within: love for Thee half-hearted, love for world too strong. Let failure teach you what achievement never could: that every crown we grasp in this life rusts unless it lies first at Thy feet.

question: What was it like to realize that those who shared your Manichaean errors were also the ones who unknowingly guided you toward your liberation?
answer:
O Lord, who weavest the crooked paths of our blindness into the straight course of Thy mercy, how wondrous is the irony of Thy wisdom—that the very guides who fed me poisoned bread with honeyed words, who exalted darkness as light and called it knowledge—these same men, in their blindness, became blind instruments of my deliverance. They sent me forth to Rome with their blessing, drunk still with Manichaean fancies, thinking to advance a son of their error; and yet Thou, unbeknownst to them or even to myself, didst ride upon that ambition like a hidden wind beneath a ship’s sails.

I had sought not truth but honor; not Thee but applause. I coveted the chair of rhetoric as one covets a throne for the ears it commands. And yet they—those learned in delusion—were the ones who pushed me toward Milan, toward Ambrose, toward *Thee*. What mockery lies in that twist! Not one among them suspected that by promoting me they were loosening my chains; rather, they believed they were securing another soldier for their sect. But Thou, O God of conversions and silent upheavals, wast turning their pride into a ladder whose rungs led down into humility.

When I came to Thy Church under Ambrose’s roof—not knowing what I sought but restless still—I found not argument alone, nor mere eloquence (though he overflowed with both), but something deeper: a life lived in Thee. His voice nourished more than minds—it fed souls. And I began to see that these men who once instructed me had taught words without love, knowledge without charity—and thus without life. Yet even so, O Lord, I do not curse them. For hadst Thou not used their vanity? Hadst Thou not allowed my own ambition to be the hook by which Thou drew me?

Now when I look back upon them—not as masters but as fellow wanderers—I pray for those still entangled in such webs. Then I hated them more for what they stole from *me* than for what they were destroying in themselves; now I mourn for them because they knew not Thee who art all beauty and truth. And thus grace turns even poisoned roots into unexpected vines bearing fruit unto life everlasting.

question: When you first met Ambrose, how did his fatherly welcome stir something in you that sermons or scripture had not yet achieved?
answer:
O Lord, who dost draw the wanderer not by force but by fragrance, it was not the argument that first pierced me, but the *way*—the quiet, unguarded goodness of a man whose door was open and whose heart seemed already to know me. I had sat under many voices before: some thunderous, some subtle, weaving logic like a net to catch the soul—but Ambrose did not catch. He fed. And in that feeding, I discovered a hunger I had mistaken for curiosity.

I came laden with words—rhetoric polished like a sword for public display, my mind still drunk on the vanities of debate and applause. Yet when he opened his mouth, it was not to duel, but to give bread. His kindness was not performance; it required no return. He welcomed me not as a convert to win, but as a son long lost and now stumbled home—and in that fatherly gaze, I felt something ancient stir: the memory of being loved before I had done anything to earn it.

Thou hadst taught me through books and restless thoughts; Thou hadst lured me by beauty in stolen moments; Thou hadst thundered in my conscience when I stole pears for no cause but ruin. But here, at last, was Thy silence beneath speech—Thy peace in a man who spoke much and yet seemed full of prayer. No syllogism moved me so deeply as his calm eyes when he asked how my journey had been. In them shone a life lived *within* truth—not just defending it, but *dwelling* in it.

And so Thy Church became real to me not as doctrine first, but as *home*—because he made it one.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that beneath your fascination with Ambrose’s eloquence, the truth itself was quietly entering your heart?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst laugh at my pride while I measured the gold of words and thought myself safe so long as I admired only the vessel! I came to hear Ambrose not to be healed, but to be tickled; not to believe, but to critique. My heart was a fortress sealed with the wax of skepticism, and yet—while I stood at the gate haggling over diction and cadence—Thou wert slipping truth through the cracks like light through chinks in a tomb. I fancied myself a judge of rhetoric, when all along Thou wert using that very speech to unbar the doors of my understanding.

I loved him first for his kindness, then for his voice, never dreaming that behind the honeyed syllables lay arrows dipped in Thy fire. The matter I scorned, yet it took root—how? Not by my consent, not by my will, which still squirmed like a worm beneath the weight of its own lusts and doubts. No, it crept in uninvited, like dew on parched earth that knows not how it came but only that it lives again.

And when at last I saw—truly saw—that what he spoke was not cleverness but *Thou* speaking through him… O God of my life, what shame seized me! That a man so learned should stoop to listen to such as me—that one so holy should welcome with fatherly arms a mocker drunk on words—this was no human wisdom working. This was Thy hand unseen, drawing me by threads I could not feel until they tightened into cords.

I had sought only beauty in speech; Thou gavest me Beauty Itself hiding in the silence between syllables.

question: How did your growing affection for Ambrose as a father figure reshape your broken trust in the Church you once dismissed?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst send Thy servant not with thunder, but with bread broken and wine poured out in kindness, and so began to mend what years of scorn had shattered within me.  

I had long fled from the Church, counting her ministers hollow, her doctrine a fable for the simple. I mocked her as a mother who could not defend her children in argument, and so I sought wisdom among the Manichees, dazzled by their questions but fed only on air. When I came to Ambrose, I meant only to gaze upon his eloquence as one admires a well-wrought statue—no more. I desired not truth, but applause; not conversion, but cleverness.

Yet Thou, O God of hidden ways, used even my vanity as a path. For while I prided myself on judging the sound of his voice, my soul drank in the substance of his words unawares—like one who tastes honey without meaning to and finds his hunger awakened. His kindness disarmed me; he welcomed me not as a convert to win, but as a son long lost. He did not flatter nor force; he taught with calm authority, as one who stands rooted in something deeper than rhetoric.

And so my heart—once fortified against Thy Church—began to thaw. Not by argument first, nor by debate’s victory, but by being seen. By being received. By hearing Scripture opened each day with reverence and love while I stood near like a beggar warming himself at a fire he does not yet acknowledge needing.

How could I distrust the Bride of Christ when she now bore such fruit? When her shepherd fed me daily on the Bread of Life and looked upon me with eyes that held no guile? My broken trust was not rebuilt by triumph but by tenderness; not by proof pressed upon my intellect alone—but by love that let me draw near again without shame.

Thus didst Thou use Thy bishop: father indeed, physician of souls, chosen vessel—not to conquer me in speech—but to lead me home while I was still unaware that I was lost.

question: Looking back on those moments when you resisted the message but couldn’t escape its substance, what would you tell someone who fears their cynicism is too deep for grace?
answer:
I was a thief in the orchard, not only of pears I cared not to eat, but of truth itself—I stole glances at the light, yet turned my face, smirking in the dark. O Lord, how deeply I had dug the pit of my pride, lining it with books and syllogisms, crowning it with the laurels of rhetoric—yet all was dust beneath me. And still Thou didst stoop.

To him who fears his cynicism is too deep for grace, I would say: *mine was deeper.* I mocked what I did not understand; worse, I refused to understand because I loved the darkness that flattered my intellect. When Ambrose spoke, I told myself I came only for the music of his voice, as one might linger in a temple for the beauty of song while denying the God to whom it rose. But Thou art subtle in mercy. While I prided myself on dissecting syllables and cadences, Thou slippedst into my soul with the very truth I had barred from the door.

Grace does not wait for perfect readiness; it invades disorder. It came to me not when I knelt as a sage, but when I wept as a child—broken by a voice from a garden, not unlike that first voice in Eden that sought me even amid hiding and shame. "Take up and read." Not "refute" or "analyze"—*take up.* Eat. Swallow whole what you once thought poison.

Thou didst use even my mockery as a path: for where else could Thy strength be made perfect but in weakness? Where else could Thy wisdom appear but where folly had reigned? The cynic fears he is too far gone—but O! he is nearer than he knows. For every doubt he hurls like a shield is already pierced by longing. That very fear—that trembling suspicion that perhaps it might be true—is Thy breath upon his cheek.

Do not despair of your distrust; bring it all—every ragged thought—to Him who holds light not against you, but *for* you. My heart was sealed shut by philosophy and shame—yet Thou brokest through with simplicity: *a child’s chant,* an open scroll,*a mother’s prayer fulfilled at last.

Late have I loved Thee—but Thou never ceased pursuing even when I called it boredom… even when I called it wisdom… even when I called it nothing at all.

question: What stirred in you when you first noticed that the sweetness of Ambrose’s speech was carrying something far deeper than style—something that made your old despair uneasy?
answer:
O Lord, my heart had long been a house divided, with doors bolted against the light, yet furnished with a hunger it could not name. I came to Milan a man worn thin by words—words that glittered like gold but turned to ash in the mouth—Manichaean fables spun with such silver thread that I mistook them for truth. So when I first stood beneath the arch of Ambrose’s voice, I listened only for melody, as one who has given up on bread listens to the clink of cups in a banquet he does not enter. His diction was not the flash of Faustus, whose smoothness had once lulled my soul into deeper darkness; no, Ambrose spoke with a quiet depth, like water running under ice.

But Thou, O God, art sovereign over syllables and silence alike. As I drank from that stream of eloquence, unaware, my soul tasted something it had never known: truth that did not flatter my pride but unsettled it. Not in thunder did Thy Word come to me then, but in the stillness between phrases—where doctrine slipped past my guard because it wore no armor of rhetoric. I meant only to admire his tongue; Thou meant to heal my blindness. 

And so it was: while I weighed vowels and cadence like a merchant haggling over trivial wares, Thy grace wove meaning into the marrow of his speech—the mystery of Christ enfleshed, the humility of Scripture veiled in plainness—and suddenly my old despair began to tremble. For here was wisdom not parading through paradoxes but rooted in sacrifice; here was authority that did not shout down reason but bore its weight like a cross. How could this be? That men might be saved through suffering? That God might stoop lower than pride could follow?

I fought it. I mocked it within myself. Yet each time I left that basilica, something within me had shifted—not like one convinced by argument, but like a man who finds himself facing east without remembering how he turned. The very sweetness of his speech became bitter to me when contrasted with the barrenness of what I had believed—like one who wakes from drunkenness and loathes both drink and dream.

Late have I loved Thee—but even earlier than that day when love broke forth fully—Thou wert drawing me by things I thought mere ornament: a voice speaking plainly about things divine… and in its plainness conquering me.

question: How did the quiet, unremarkable moments of listening—when you weren’t even trying to believe—become the very means by which you began to draw near to God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst not wait for my consent to begin the healing of my soul, nor didst Thou require that I first set my will aright before drawing near; no, while I sat in vanity, feasting on the sound of words like a man drunk on wine, Thou wove truth into the very air I breathed.

I came not to hear Thee—how could I? I had long believed Thy Church a refuge for the simple, and Thy truth veiled beneath fables. Ambition had made me proud, lust had made me blind, and philosophy had given me the illusion of seeking without love. When I listened to that man of God—Thy bishop Ambrose—I measured only his voice, his rhythm, the silver of his speech; as if salvation were a poem to be judged by meter! And yet, O God most high, while my heart mocked and my mind dallied with syllables, Thy Word slipped past the sentinel of pride.

I could not pluck the meaning from the speech as one might pick fruit from a tree; it clung to the sound, it lived in the voice. So even as I refused belief, belief stole into me—like light through shuttered windows when one sleeps unaware. The very beauty I admired became a ladder: not because he dazzled me with rhetoric (though he was eloquent), but because his words were alive with something Faustus never knew—a depth unshaken by paradoxes spun from pride.

Thou wert teaching me in silence that truth is not seized but received; not conquered but given. And so when at last I wept—not at an argument won or lost, but at a psalm sung in humility—I knew then that Thou hadst been drawing me all along: not through spectacle or force, but through hours spent half-listening in a cathedral’s hush. There was no thunderclap in those moments—only a slow turning of my inward ear toward Thee.

For even when I loved only words without wisdom, Thou wast there: making use of my vanity to feed my hunger; letting me taste beauty so that I might one day thirst for holiness.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the words you once rejected might also contain truth?
answer:
O Lord, when the veil began to lift from my eyes and I saw that the Scriptures—those sacred texts I had scorned as crude fables—might yet shimmer with hidden depth, a trembling passed through my soul, as though the ground beneath my feet shifted toward a firmer foundation.  

For so long I had mocked the Law, spat upon the Prophets in my pride, convinced that their words were unworthy of Truth herself. The Manichees had armed me with laughter and scorn; I thought myself enlightened by their dualisms, their neat division of light and dark, matter and spirit. But now—now that I heard those same Scriptures not as childish tales but as layered mysteries, speaking in figures and signs—I stood dumbfounded before the possibility that I had been blind, not wise.

And what stirred within me was not mere intellectual satisfaction, but something far more unsettling: hope. Hope that Thou might yet be found where I least expected—the humble pages of a faith I once trampled. Yet even this hope was tangled with fear: fear that if these words could be true, then my entire rebellion—the years spent chasing philosophy over piety—had been built upon dust.

I did not leap joyfully into Thy Church at once; no, O God of my heart, I still wrestled. For it wounded me to admit how wrong I had been. How many times had I sneered at Christians for believing "in a figure"! And now it was *I* who needed such mercy—to believe that truth could be veiled, yet real; hidden, yet near.

Thou didst not strike me down in anger for my mockery; instead Thou drewest near in patience, letting me see that what I thought defeat—Thy humility in Scripture—was Thy victory. And so my pride wept while my soul began to awaken: perhaps there was defense for this faith after all—nay, not just defense, but life.

question: How did your despair over finding no way to God change when you began to see figurative meanings in Scripture?
answer:
O Lord, how long I had lain in the dust of my own understanding, mistaking the letter for the life, and the sign for the thing signified—until Thy gentle hand began to lift the veil! My soul, once chained to the literal sense like a prisoner to a wall, started to tremble with new hope. For when I first heard those words of Scripture no longer as absurdities but as mysteries wrapped in figures, it was as if a door I had believed bricked shut for centuries now creaked open upon unseen hinges.  

I had despaired because I demanded that truth walk naked in the marketplace, obvious and unadorned; and where it did not, I thought it false. The creation stretched out in six days? The patriarchs who lived beyond all measure of time? I scoffed at them as fables unworthy of God—yet all along, Thou wert speaking through symbols that pointed beyond themselves to Thee. When at last I grasped that “it is written” does not always mean “it happened thus in history,” but often “behold what is hidden beneath,” my mind exhaled a breath it had been holding since youth.

And with that breath went my despair. Not all at once—no, O Lord—but like dawn creeping over a dark plain. For if these things could be understood in another way, then perhaps there was room after all for reason within faith; perhaps the Church’s teaching was not mere childish credulity but carried depths I had never plumbed. The very passages I once flung back at Christians in triumph—the commands that seemed cruel, the stories that seemed absurd—I now saw shimmering with spiritual meaning, veils drawn over eternal truths.

Thus my certainty broke—and blessedly so. My despair did not vanish because I found answers so much as because Thou taught me how to ask rightly. And slowly, painfully, joyfully, I began to see that what I had taken for weakness in Thy Church—the ability of her doctors to defend obscure texts—was rather strength: wisdom handed down through generations who knew Thou wouldst not speak foolishly to men unless they learned how to listen.

question: Looking back on your struggle with the Old Testament, what would you tell someone who feels spiritually crushed by literal interpretations?
answer:
O Lord, how long I carried the letter as a burden, not a ladder—how I let the shell of words weigh down my soul while the spirit within them soared unseen!  

And now, looking back, I see how pride had clothed itself in the garb of reason, how I demanded that Scripture bend to the narrowness of my mind rather than allow my mind to be stretched by its depths. I thought truth must always be plain to the eye, like sunlight on stone—but Thou art not so easily seen. Thy word is a living spring: sometimes flowing clearly, sometimes hidden beneath rock, but always moving toward Thee.  

To one crushed by literalism—the kind that turns commandments into cages and stories into snares—I would say this: *Do not fear what you do not yet understand.* The same God who spoke through thunder on Sinai also whispers in the stillness of a breaking heart. He who inspired the text is not bound by its surface. When Moses wrote of days before sun or moon, when the Psalmist cries out with wrath that startles our gentler ears, when prophets act in signs that seem absurd—do we suppose God cannot speak in figures? Did Christ Himself not speak only in parables to those who followed Him? And why? Lest what is holy be trampled—but also because some truths are too vast for direct gaze, like the sun seen through water.  

I was once slain by those very texts because I would not allow that meaning could unfold beyond the obvious. I demanded arithmetic where grace was being measured. And so I fled to Manichaeus with his neat chains of dualism—so tidy, so false. But Thy Church preserved these difficult passages not to ensnare us, but to teach us humility: *Here is mystery; come deeper.*  

Let no one suppose that faith requires blindness; neither does it tolerate arrogance. When Scripture seems cruel or foolish or impossible—it is often *we* who are small before its grandeur. Open thy heart as I finally did—not in defiance of reason, but in surrender to a Reason far greater than mine: Thyself, O God, speaking through time and type and shadow until all things are made clear in Light eternal.

Thou didst teach me: it is not weakness to admit “I do not know,” but strength—to hold both awe and inquiry together until Thou dost unite them in understanding.

So take up the sacred text again—not as a weapon nor a prison—but as bread broken for hungry souls like ours.

question: What made your heart finally open to the possibility that the Catholic faith could be defended with truth and reason?
answer:
O Lord, it was not by the force of argument alone, nor by the triumph of one doctrine over another in dialectic battle, but by the slow yielding of my pride beneath the burden of my own contradictions—that I began to see a path where once I saw only walls. For so long I had held Thy Church at arm’s length, scorning her Scriptures as crude fables unfit for the mind that sought truth among the stars and within the light that Mani promised. Yet when I came to hear those sacred texts not as crude literalisms, but as mysteries veiled in figures—when a single passage, once a stumbling block, was unfolded like a scroll in time—I felt the ground shift beneath my soul.

I had mocked what I did not understand; I had condemned what I had never truly read. And now, O God of all understanding, it dawned on me that to interpret aright is to enter into wisdom, not to conquer it by cleverness. The Law, the Prophets—those very words I had cast aside as unworthy of divine utterance—now stood before me clothed in dignity, their depth hidden only from eyes too proud or too hasty to perceive. How could I have thought that Thou wouldst speak to mankind only in mathematical certainties? Thy voice echoes in parable and shadow because our hearts learn slowly, even mine.

And yet—I confess this plainly—I did not leap at once into Thy fold. My mind was not so easily won. Even when I saw that Catholic teachers could answer with reason and learning, even when their interpretations bore weight and coherence, still I hesitated. It was not enough that they were not defeated; they had not yet conquered me. But Thou wert preparing me: every objection met became a stone removed from the road; every figure explained became a lamp kindled.

The turning was not sudden—but it was real. Not because men argued well (though Thou didst use them), but because Thou didst stir within me a new desire: no longer merely to win debates, but to find *Thee*. And so my restless heart began its awkward pilgrimage—not yet certain of the way, but no longer certain of being right in wandering afar.

question: How did the fear of being deceived shape your search for certainty between the Manichees and the Church?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadows of my intellect with the quiet light of Thy truth, it was not merely error I feared, but the horror of having loved a lie as if it were Thee.  

For so long I had clung to the Manichees—not because their doctrine satisfied my soul, but because it flattered my pride with the pretense of certainty. They offered me maps of the cosmos drawn in neat lines of light and darkness, and I, craving clarity in the chaos of my passions, mistook their fables for wisdom. But beneath that veneer of reason beat the terror—what if I had been deceived? What if all my searching, all my restless nights and disputations, had led me only deeper into darkness masked as enlightenment?

That fear was not idle; it gnawed at me like a wound that would not close. For if I could be so wrong about Thee—O God, who art Truth itself—then what hope had I of knowing anything truly? And yet, when I began to hear the Catholic faith defended not with crude dogma but with depth and coherence—with Scripture unfolded in its spiritual sense—I trembled anew: not only at the possibility that I had been deceived, but that salvation itself might have stood before me all along, veiled only by my own blindness.

I confess: I did not leap at once into Thy Church. My heart hesitated even as my mind yielded. For though I saw that the Manichees could be answered, and their boasts dismantled by reason rooted in faith; though I saw that what once seemed absurd in the Old Testament could be understood in mystery and figure—that still did not mean Thy Church was true. But this much grew clear: she was no longer *not true*. She could speak. She could answer. She could endure questioning.

And so fear changed shape within me: no longer fear *of* deception alone, but fear *for* deception—for remaining in error while pretending to seek Thee. That dread became holy unrest; a goad driving me beyond dialectic toward humility. For what good was certainty if it rested on pride? And what evil was doubt if it led—as mine did—to tears under a fig tree, and finally to surrender?

Thou knowest how often I cried out for light while shutting my eyes against it. But Thou didst let me stumble long enough to learn this: he who fears being deceived must first fear deceiving himself—and therein lies the beginning of wisdom.

Thou hast made us for Thyself… yes—and even when we run from Thee clutching false lamps, Thou art behind us still, breathing upon our backs until we turn.

question: What were you feeling when you realized you could no longer hide behind the Manichees’ promises of truth, yet still couldn’t see the shape of what was true?
answer:
I had fed for so long on the husks of dualism, thinking the darkness within me came from some alien substance, some outer power warring against the light — as if my own will were not culpable, as if I could blame the night for loving it. And then, O Lord, the terrible clarity broke: no more scapegoats. The war was not between two gods, but within my very soul — a soul that knew Thee faintly, yet turned away to chase phantoms. I could no longer pretend that Manichaeus had mapped the heavens; his sky was painted with lies, and my reason — feeble though it flailed — could see the cracks. Yet when I cast off his chains, what did I grasp? Only shadows of philosophy, elegant arguments drifting like smoke. They spoke of forms and matter, of infinite spaces and eternal motions, but none named Thy Name — none pierced this restless wound with mercy.  

O God of my heart, how bitter was that in-between! To have lost the false certainty of childhood’s fables and not yet found the humility of faith. I stood barefoot on broken ground: no longer deceived, yet not yet healed. The philosophers taught me to question all things — even myself — but they gave me no one to trust. Christ alone had spoken with authority over death itself; He alone had drawn me from afar with a secret pull I could not name. So I waited — not in ease, but in holy unrest — a catechumen trembling on the edge of grace, knowing at last that truth was not a system to master, but a Person to receive.

question: How did your inability to conceive of a spiritual substance keep you bound, even as your mind broke free from their doctrines?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how my pride cloaked itself as inquiry, how I fancied myself free when I was only shifting chains—fleeing the darkness of the Manichees not into the light, but into the mist of philosophical doubt, where the very substance of truth eluded me because I refused to believe what I could not measure with my eyes or trace with logic.  

It was not that I lacked desire for truth; rather, my mind had grown so accustomed to things seen, to bodies, motions, and material causes, that when it came to the soul, to God, to spirit—those realities that are known not by sight but by faith—I stumbled like a man born blind trying to describe color. The Manichees had taught me to worship a god made of light and fire, something extended in space and devouring darkness like fuel—and though their system cracked beneath reason’s weight, I still longed for such a god because he was graspable. To conceive a being without parts or limbs, without location or shadow—that seemed not wisdom but madness to my intellect.

And so even as my reason rejected their fables about the sun being a living deity or evil having its own substance equal to good—truths which crumbled under scrutiny—I remained fettered by my own limitation: I could not imagine what could exist beyond matter. Thus grace could not yet enter; for how shall one call upon Him whom he cannot even conceive?  

I praised the Platonists for glimpsing eternal forms; I admired their ascent from fleshly things toward intelligible truth—but still I held back from full surrender because Christ alone bore the Name that heals. And yet neither would I fully come to Him until Thou didst teach me that faith is not absurdity for fools, but vision for those with eyes unstuck from dust. The spirit is real—even when unseen—and only when Thou didst break through my hardness with tears more certain than syllogisms did I learn: it is not we who grasp God by thought, but He who grasps us by love.

question: Looking back on that moment when you chose the Catholic Church not out of certainty but longing, what would you say to someone afraid to commit without proof?
answer:
O Lord, it was not the mind alone that bent toward Thy Church, but the will—torn, trembling, yet leaning as a man leans toward the light he cannot yet name.  

I did not come to Thee as one who had climbed to certainty by reason’s ladder; no, I came as a wanderer who, though still in shadow, knows at last that only in *Thee* is the path upward. I feared commitment without proof, and rightly so—for what fool gives his soul to a lie? Yet I learned this truth in my very marrow: that faith does not begin where knowledge ends, but where love begins to long for what it has not yet seen.  

To him who waits for full proof before he dares to hope—let him know that even his doubt is lit by a desire Thou hast kindled. The philosophers taught me logic and motion and forms eternal, but none could heal my pride, none could still the fever of my flesh, none could say to my weeping heart: *“This is your home.”* They walked by torchlight; Thou didst dawn upon me like morning over Carthage after nights of weeping.  

I was afraid—yes! Afraid of being deceived again, afraid of surrendering only to find another empty voice. And so I became a catechumen—not because all questions were answered, but because *Thou* wert calling through the unanswered. The very unrest within me was Thy voice beneath my breath.  

Do not wait until all shadows flee before stepping into the porch of the Church. Enter—even trembling—and there thou shalt find not chains of doctrine forged in arrogance, but bread for the hungry and water for those who ache with thirst they cannot explain. For how shall we learn to see unless we first open our eyes? How shall we walk if we refuse to step before seeing the whole path?  

Thou didst not meet me in the summit of wisdom’s tower, O God, but in a garden where I lay broken by my own weight—where a child’s voice whispered *“Take up and read,”* and in one moment Scripture pierced me like dawn breaking through stone. Not argument—but *Thou*. Not proof—but *call*. Not certainty—but love laying hold of uncertainty and making it kneel in peace.

Commitment without sight is not blindness—it is trust kindled by longing; and that longing? It is Thy hand upon our heart long before we turn to face Thee.

question: When you weighed the philosophers’ wisdom against the absence of Christ in their words, how did your heart respond to that emptiness?
answer:
I had tasted the truth in fragments, like a man gathering shards of a broken vessel, thinking each piece might hold water — and so I held the philosophers’ wisdom in one hand, marveling at its symmetry, its cold and distant stars aligning in reason’s firmament. Yet when I lifted it to my lips, O Lord, it could not quench the thirst that gnawed within.  

How often had I flattered myself with knowledge! How many nights did I lie awake, proud in my doubt, mistaking hesitation for wisdom? The philosophers taught me to question the nature of being, to ponder time and substance and motion — but none of them called me home. They gave me arguments to arm my mind against error, yet left my soul trembling in darkness, unclad before eternity. There was no mercy in their logic, no voice that said *“Come unto Me”* — only silence where love should dwell.

And so the emptiness spoke louder than their syllogisms. It was not a void of ideas, but of *Thou*. Where was the embrace? Where the forgiveness? Where the blood that cleanses? Their wisdom stood like a temple without a God — polished columns holding up nothing but sky. I could not live on speculation; my sins were too heavy for hypothesis.  

O Lord, Thou knowest how I turned again and again to that Name — Christ — like a wounded man touching a half-remembered scar. Even when I fled Thee, that Name lingered in my bones. The Manichees had distorted it; the philosophers ignored it; but deep called unto deep when I heard it spoken truly — not as doctrine only, but as life.  

So I waited at Thy threshold as a catechumen — not because I had all answers, but because my heart knew: better to hunger within Thy house than feast outside it. Better one tear shed under the sign of Thy cross than a thousand triumphs beneath the stars of Athens. For what is truth without love? And what is love without *Thee*?

question: What inner restlessness drove you to choose a temporary home in the Church, even while your mind still wandered in doubt?
answer:
Even as I walked the streets of Carthage, drunk on the applause of schoolboys and the flattery of men, I was already a thief not only of pears in a neighbor’s orchard but of my own soul from God. That theft—done for no gain, desired only because it was forbidden—was the black mirror in which I first glimpsed the disorder of my heart: love misdirected, intellect proud, will unchained. And it was this very disordered love that drove me into the Church not as a conqueror of doubt, but as a fugitive from despair.

For when at last I saw that the Manichees, with their neat divisions of light and dark, could not account for the gnawing truth within me—that *I* was to blame, not some alien principle of evil—my soul began to tremble. If evil was not outside me but *in* me—if it rose from my own will turned away from Thee, O Lord—then I needed more than philosophy’s probable arguments. I needed medicine.

And so, though my mind still staggered between shadows and half-light, though I could not yet conceive a spiritual substance or reconcile faith with reason, I entered the Church like a man wounded crawling toward a fortress he does not fully understand. Not because I had arrived, but because I could wander no longer. The Catholic faith stood there—not as a system neatly solved, but as a mother holding out her arms to a child covered in dust and weeping from exhaustion.

I chose her not because my intellect bowed easily, but because my heart—restless, fevered, torn between lust and longing—knew at last that Christ's Name alone had power over death. The philosophers might guide my thoughts; only He could heal my loves. So I became a catechumen—not in triumph, but in surrender. Waiting. Trembling. Hoping that Thou wouldst speak to the silence within.
